data.title: Europe’s misguided energy policy is crushing industry
data.description:
Over the past decade, Europe’s weak economic performance has been blamed on familiar shocks: the global financial crisis, the eurozone debt crisis, Brexit and ageing populations.

Each have played a role – yet none explains why growth outcomes within Europe itself are now diverging so sharply.

Across the Continent, countries with access to cheap, reliable electricity are pulling ahead while those burdened with structurally high power costs are falling behind. Growth is weakening in Europe’s large core economies, notably the UK and Germany, while momentum is stronger in parts of the Nordics, Iberia and eastern Europe.

The result increasingly resembles a “doughnut” economy: strength at the edges, weakness at the centre.

This divergence reflects a widening competitiveness gap driven by differences in energy economics and above all, the cost of electricity.

At the turn of the millennium, electricity prices for households and industry in the US, the UK and Germany were broadly comparable.

Today, industrial electricity prices in the UK and Germany are four to five times higher than in the United States and materially higher than in other parts of Europe such as the Nordics and Iberia. Household prices are two to three times higher.

This is not a temporary distortion caused by the war in Ukraine or volatile global gas markets. Gas prices have largely normalised. Electricity prices have not.

The explanation lies in the structure of Europe’s energy systems and the policy choices that underpin them. In countries such as the UK and Germany, decarbonisation has relied heavily on capital-intensive, intermittent generation – particularly offshore wind – within electricity markets. While offshore wind has helped reduce emissions, it has also imposed large, system-wide costs.

These include long-term subsidy commitments, extensive grid reinforcement, curtailment and the backup generation and storage required to stabilise supply. Crucially, many of these costs are locked in for decades.

The economic consequences are hard to overstate.

High electricity costs act like a tax on the economy: on industry, on services and on households. Energy-intensive businesses face a stark choice – relocate or shut down. Industries that depend on abundant, cheap power, such as advanced manufacturing and AI data centres, will naturally gravitate towards lower-cost jurisdictions.

At the same time, higher household energy bills reduce disposable income, weighing on consumption and growth. The result is a slow but persistent erosion of competitiveness.

The critical point is that high electricity prices are not an inevitable feature of decarbonisation. This is not an argument against climate science or the need to reduce emissions, but the way the energy transition is executed matters enormously.

Historical evidence documented by thinkers such as Vaclav Smil, author of the magisterial book Energy and Civilization (which should be required reading for any selected MP entering the House of Commons), shows that cheap and abundant energy is a critical – perhaps the single most important – foundation for economic growth and development.

Too often, energy policy has been shaped by political and institutional incentives rather than by system-level engineering considerations, with insufficient attention paid to total system costs and their implications for national competitiveness.

Within Europe, there are clear examples of more pragmatic, engineering-led approaches.

In the Nordic countries, industrial electricity prices are close to US levels. Norway, Sweden and Finland benefit from vast hydroelectric resources, built decades ago and largely paid for.

They have also taken a pragmatic approach to nuclear power, retaining and in some cases expanding capacity rather than shutting it down. Combined with ample land for low-cost onshore wind, this mix delivers cheap and reliable electricity.

The gap in electricity prices between the Nordics and countries such as the UK and Germany is large, structural and unlikely to close any time soon.

Cheaper, more reliable power lowers operating costs across the economy, encourages capital investment and supports more energy-intensive activity, feeding through into higher productivity and faster growth over time.

Spain and Portugal offer a different but equally instructive model. Their energy strategies have been more balanced, retaining nuclear power and maintaining diversified gas supply routes. Crucially, they are heavily leveraged to solar power both at utility scale and on rooftops.

Solar is a deflationary technology: its costs continue to fall and its system-wide economics are fundamentally different from offshore wind. As a result, electricity prices in Spain and Portugal sit well below those in the UK, with clear implications for competitiveness and growth.

These differences are increasingly visible in economic performance. While forecasts vary, the pattern is consistent: the Nordics, Iberia and parts of eastern Europe are growing faster and more in line with economies that enjoy access to cheaper power.

Weak productivity and sluggish GDP growth in countries such as the UK and Germany are often attributed to abstract macroeconomic forces or one-off political events. But there is a simpler and more material explanation: years of rising electricity costs have quietly but steadily undermined competitiveness.

For investors and policymakers alike, the lesson is clear. Europe is not a single, homogenous economic bloc. It is a patchwork of countries with sharply diverging energy economics and increasingly diverging growth trajectories.

Where electricity is cheap and reliable, investment follows, productivity rises and growth proves more resilient. Where power is expensive and volatile, capital quietly goes elsewhere. Over time, these differences compound.

Europe’s emerging “doughnut” economy is not an accident, nor is it inevitable – but unless energy policy is re-anchored in system-wide economics as well as climate goals, the Continent risks locking in a form of structural stagnation.

Niall Gallagher is European equities investment manager at Jupiter Asset Management.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Chinese carmakers cash in on Britain’s EV credits
data.description:
Chinese carmakers are expected to cash in on Britain’s net zero mandate after it emerged that major manufacturers will have to pay rivals to avoid fines.

Eight carmakers including Jaguar Land Rover and Toyota did not sell enough electric cars or low-emission vehicles last year to meet government mandates, according to an industry analysis.

Four of them – Suzuki, Nissan, Mazda and Honda – are far enough behind the target that they will have to acquire “credits” from companies that have significantly exceeded the targets.

Those in the best position to sell credits include manufacturers that sell large numbers of electric vehicles such as Tesla, Chinese champion BYD and Polestar and MG, owned by China’s Geely and SAIC respectively.

Car industry experts have criticised the mandate as “subsidising” the Chinese industry since many manufacturers importing from China are selling exclusively electric vehicles (EV), so have a surplus of credits to offload.

The zero-emission vehicle (ZEV) mandate, brought in by the Conservatives and modified by Labour last year, requires a proportion of each manufacturer’s cars each year to be electric. It rises every year from 22pc in 2024 to 80pc in 2030.

Manufacturers receive allowances helping them make up the target for selling low-emission cars such as hybrids. Others will be able to “borrow” against future years in the expectation that they will surpass the target towards the end of the decade.

However, borrowing against future years is capped – meaning those far enough below the target will have to acquire credits to avoid fines of £12,000 per vehicle below the target.

Last year, when the target was 28pc, almost all carmakers missed the quota purely on the basis of EV sales alone, according to a report from the non-profit Transport & Environment group.

While several were able to make it up with low-emission credits, Hyundai, Stellantis, Toyota and JLR would have to borrow against future EV sales. Suzuki, Nissan, Mazda and Honda will have to acquire credits even after maxing out borrowing against future years.

Between them, the four Japanese manufacturers are 4,273 vehicles behind the target, according to the analysis – and so could risk fines of around £50m collectively if they fail to acquire credits.

Tim Dexter, of Transport & Environment, said manufacturers might acquire credits through production or licensing deals with other carmakers.

Nissan, for example, has pooled credits with Renault to avoid EU fines in the past. Last year, Nissan struck a similar deal with BYD.

Matthias Schmidt, an independent automotive analyst, said: “Tesla, Volvo, Polestar and Chinese manufacturers such as BYD are likely to be the biggest beneficiaries of this as the companies that have most exceeded the target.”

Nissan, which is manufacturing its new Leaf EV in the UK, has argued that the ZEV mandate is outdated and fails to take into account slowing EV demand. Suzuki has discontinued certain models in the UK in an attempt to get close to the targets.

The four manufacturers were contacted for comment. 

A Department for Transport spokesman said: “Our Electric Car Grant has helped over 50,000 drivers go electric and save up to £3,750 off the price of a new EV – helping families keep more of their cash whilst also helping industry to hit their sales targets.

“We’re supercharging the UK’s charging network by backing councils to install 100,000 more public charge points and cutting the cost of installing home chargers.

“Industry is on track to meet their EV targets and last year we introduced flexibilities so manufacturers can meet these in multiple ways, not just through sales.”

data.title: ‘Shadow banks’ quizzed over meltdown threat from hidden losses
data.description:
The $2tn (£1.5tn) private credit industry is being quizzed by the City watchdog over fears its unrecognised losses could fuel a financial system meltdown, The Telegraph can reveal.

Officials from the Financial Conduct Authority have in recent weeks been piling pressure on so-called shadow banks – an increasingly critical source of funding for the speculative AI boom – to more rigorously mark down the value of loans that are at risk of not being repaid in full.

The FCA’s confidential discussions were revealed by City sources amid growing concern about the rapid rise of private credit funds, which compete with traditional banks to make loans to companies. Private credit assets will be worth almost $4tn by 2030, according to a recent report by Moody’s.

Critics of the shadow banks have accused them of “mark-to-myth” valuations that readily account for unrealised gains but rarely take hits even as a loan is obviously turning bad. This is in contrast to “mark-to-market” accounting, which is seen as more transparent and rigorous but can mean unwanted turbulence for investors.

Since the last financial crisis such lending has become a lucrative source of fee growth for some of the world’s biggest money managers including Ares Management, Blackstone, Apollo Global Management and the Carlyle Group.

The industry has accelerated rapidly in the past few years. A flood of cash from Gulf sovereign wealth funds seeking higher returns than are available on public markets has met massive demand for loans from data centre builders betting the AI boom will turn profitable. Figures from private equity database Preqin suggest that about $50bn of data centre projects were funded by private markets between 2021 and May 2025.

The FCA declined to comment on which of the major players in private credit it had approached about their loan valuation practices. There is no suggestion of malpractice by any individual provider.

Sources said the FCA was not focused on loans issued to a particular sector, but on giving extra scrutiny to lending to critical businesses such as utilities.

However, Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, recently warned of an “urgent need” to make the shadow banking industry safer amid growing fears it could be the cause of the next financial crisis.

Funds often themselves borrow money from traditional banks, exposing the wider system to risk.

Pensions risk

US domestic banks have now lent $1.2tn to shadow banks, according to ratings agency Moody’s, which estimated that $300bn of these loans were to private credit providers.

Wall Street banks have emerged as among the staunchest financial supporters of the private credit industry, with the volume of their loans to private debt funds soaring 145pc from 2020 to 2025, according to a recent report by the Federal Reserve.

Their hunt for cash to lend has more recently drawn in retail investors and insurers, stoking fears that ordinary pensioners could face losses in a sudden reckoning in which providers are unable to quickly return cash.

This risk is understood to be driving FCA action. Officials are collaborating closely on private credit loan valuation practices with the Bank of England and its international peers such as the Securities and Exchange Commission in the US.

Last week BlackRock, the world’s biggest money manager, marked down a large US private credit fund by 19pc. It is unknown whether the unusual step was taken in response to regulatory pressure and BlackRock has declined to comment on the action, which sent tremors through Wall Street.

Politicians are also increasingly concerned about the dangers of shadow banking. In an echo of the financial crisis, US senators last summer raised concerns about credit ratings agencies. They were accused of providing cover for inflated loan valuations and being rewarded for playing along.

The FCA is also understood to be looking at how private credit firms rely on ratings agencies to issue loans.

The House of Lords’ financial services regulation committee has singled out the Treasury for its “passivity” in the face of the possible risks from shadow banking, adding that the Government had “demonstrated a limited grasp” of the potential risks from shadow banking.

The Treasury said that in recent years it had worked together with regulators to significantly increase its focus on the non-banks sectors and that it has a robust, flexible framework to protect financial stability.

Cracks emerge

Howard Marks, the legendary distressed-debt investor, said last year that he did not believe private credit was a risk to the financial system.

But he added: “The tide has never gone out on private credit, meaning we haven’t had an opportunity to see its flaws. As far as I’m concerned, the main one is the possibility that some managers have been in such a hurry to scoop up capital and put it to work – so they could come back for more – that they relaxed their credit standards and failed to demand a sufficient margin of safety.

“If there’s ever another difficult period in the economy and the market, we’ll see the result.”

Glimpses of overvalued private credit have begun to emerge. The bankruptcies of the car finance provider Tricolor and car-parts supplier First Brands both imposed multibillion-dollar losses on the private credit industry seemingly out of the blue, albeit amid allegations of fraud.

The Telegraph last year reported concerns about borrowing by the troubled broadband provider TalkTalk from the private credit giant Ares. While TalkTalk’s listed bonds were trading at a fraction of their original value, it was not clear that Ares had marked its £500m lending to the market.

It is understood that Ares evaluates loans on at least a quarterly basis and validates them with third party agencies, which are also audited annually.

Michelle Beck, the director of markets at the FCA, said: “Timely and reliable valuations help ensure that private markets work well and continue to support investment and growth in the UK. 

“We expect firms to be able to reassess valuations when material developments or changes in conditions occur.”

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Lloyds boss defends banks after Farage threatens tax raid
data.description:
The chief executive of Lloyds has defended Britain’s banking sector after Nigel Farage threatened to tax lenders if Reform wins power.

Charlie Nunn said that any attempts to weaken banks risk unintended consequences for the economy, pointing to the key sources of lending that Lloyds provides to households and businesses.

He said: “A strong economy needs strong banks.

“We’re very committed to increasing access to first-time buyers, increasing access to borrowing and lending to SMEs [small and medium-sized enterprises] to drive growth. We need a strong banking sector to support that.”

It is the first time that Mr Nunn has responded publicly to Mr Farage’s threats.

The Reform UK leader has claimed his party would scrap interest payments that British lenders receive through the Bank of England’s quantitative easing programme.

Speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos last week, Mr Farage sought to justify Reform’s proposed policy by saying he doesn’t like banks because his account had been closed by Coutts.

Reform has claimed the policy would bring in up to £40bn for taxpayers.

Mr Farage said: “We are going to do it. Some of the banks won’t like it. Well, I don’t like the banks very much because they debanked me, didn’t they?

“This will be tough for banks to accept, but I am sorry, the drain on public finances is just too great. It’s not a tax. They are just not going to get free money any more. They’ll adjust; business always does.”

Despite hailing the importance of UK banks, the Lloyds boss sought to shrug off Mr Farage’s threats by claiming it would not have a significant impact on his business.

“We actually have a minimal amount of our deposits at the Bank of England,” he said. “So for Lloyds Banking Group, this isn’t a major issue for us.”

Mr Nunn’s comments came as Lloyds reported a 12pc jump in pre-tax profits to £6.7bn for 2025, ahead of analyst expectations of around £6.4bn. This cleared the way for a £1.75bn share buyback programme.

The better-than-expected results were also driven in part by strong revenues from its insurance and wealth divisions.

In the last quarter of 2025, almost a third of its income came from these new sources, justifying a recent diversification push to protect the bank from falling interest rates.

Lloyds also hailed £50m in savings from using AI to automate HR and other tasks.

data.title: Industry sacrificed over net zero ideology, says Siemens Energy boss
data.description:
Europe is sacrificing industry to net zero “ideology”, the chairman of Siemens Energy has warned.

Joe Kaeser said strict zero-emission targets had crippled manufacturing and heavy industry in Germany and beyond. He called on the German government to work on a more “economically feasible plan” for the country to decarbonise.

“The critics have a point when they say you risk your economic power [and] society’s wellbeing by being ideological about net zero,” he said.

His comments will raise questions about Britain’s approach to net zero, which official forecasts show will add hundreds of billions of pounds to government spending over the next few decades.

As well as questions about cost, the UK and Europe face increasing pressure from the US to dial back plans which Trump administration officials claim are making countries “subservient” to Beijing.

Sir Keir Starmer will fly to China this week in the first visit by a British prime minister since 2018, with energy likely to be among the topics discussed.

Mr Kaeser, who previously ran German industrial giant Siemens before becoming chairman of its energy division, warned that European companies were becoming uncompetitive because of green energy policies.

He criticised the government in Berlin for forcing zero emission targets on carmakers and demanding that businesses prove the products they sell have no connection to deforestation.

The German businessman also criticised officials for sacrificing reliable, cheap energy by ditching fossil fuels.

Mr Kaeser said: “They became ideologists on renewables. That’s a bad idea. If you are an industrial country and cannot provide a sustainable, affordable and reliable source of energy, you have a problem.”

Germany’s share of global emissions is currently less than 2pc – much lower than other industrialised countries including Brazil and India – and Mr Kaeser said even halving this figure would make little difference to the global picture.

By comparison, China now accounts for a third of global carbon emissions while the UK’s share is less than 1pc.

“Why would you risk your industrial competitiveness by maybe being a rounding error in the global discussion?” Mr Kaeser said.

Germany has a goal of hitting zero carbon emissions by 2045, a more aggressive target than Britain’s legally enshrined 2050 deadline.

Mr Kaeser, who is also the chairman of Daimler trucks, attacked the EU’s approach of reaching net zero by regulation. He said Brussels spent too much time drawing up rules for manufacturers without considering how they could kill demand.

Under current targets, 43pc of Europe’s heavy goods vehicle fleet must be electric by 2030, 65pc by 2035 and 90pc by 2040. Manufacturers who fail to comply face huge fines.

“The EU regulates everything,” Mr Kaeser said. “It regulates the supply side. So the EU tells us ... we don’t want the diesel truck any more. They don’t go to the customer and ask the demand side, what are you up to? What does your business case look like?”

The Siemens Energy chairman was speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, where world leaders and business elites gathered this week. While much of the event was dominated by Donald Trump’s threats over Greenland, US officials also used the summit to criticise Europe’s net zero policies.

Mr Trump used his speech to attendees to rail against “the green new scam”.

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, warned that Europe was becoming “subservient” to China because it was reliant on Beijing for products such as electric car batteries.

Most of Britain’s solar panels currently come from China and Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, is also driving a rapid expansion of UK wind power. Chinese companies dominate in the production of wind turbines.

Siemens Energy, which operates a factory in Hull, is still dealing with the fallout from a crisis in its wind division caused by quality issues and soaring production costs. The disaster forced the German government to bail the company out in 2023. Mr Kaeser said the company remained committed to turning the unit around and making it profitable again.

By contrast, orders for its gas turbines are booming as demand for AI data centres grows.

data.title: The German plot to woo Rolls-Royce
data.description:
Sitting in front of an enormous prototype jet engine, a German minister beams at the camera as he signs a cooperation deal with two top Rolls-Royce executives.

“With this, both sides commit to actively shape the transformation of aviation,” a social media post boasted gleefully afterwards.

Dietmar Woidke, the president of the German state of Brandenburg, had every reason to feel cheerful at November’s signing ceremony.

In the past decade alone, his state has attracted hundreds of millions of pounds in investment from Rolls and positioned itself as a “centre of excellence” for small-business jet engines.

Now it is jockeying for an even bigger prize – and a chance to swipe some of Britain’s industrial crown jewels.

In recent months, German officials have mounted a charm offensive to secure more work related to Rolls’ next-generation jet engine, the UltraFan.

Rolls-Royce Dahlewitz hub, outside Berlin, has already played a key role in developing what is arguably the new turbofan’s key innovation: its gearbox.

But the Germans are understood to be pushing hard for Dahlewitz to play an even larger role in the future, based on an offer of potentially generous subsidies.

The timing of these lobbying efforts is not coincidental either, coming as Berlin scrambles to defend itself from an avalanche of Chinese cars that threaten to crush Germany’s once-unstoppable motor industry.

Worryingly for Sir Keir Starmer, Rolls appears to be listening.

While the company is understood to favour keeping development in Britain, insiders insist it is duty-bound to consider all options – particularly as it is accountable to shareholders, not the Labour Government in London.

One well-placed industry source said: “The Germans are already involved in UltraFan. The only question now is what they do beyond that.”

Such a loss of investment by the company, widely regarded as a national champion, would deal a serious blow to Starmer’s industrial strategy and risk the loss of valuable know-how to foreign factories.

“It’s a very sensible thing for Rolls-Royce to consider – and potentially very bad for the UK,” warns Rian Whitton, an industry analyst at Bismarck Analysis.

The German advances have come at a critical moment, just as Rolls is considering a return to the fast-growing market for “narrow-body” planes, which have a single aisle and are used by carriers such as Ryanair for short-haul flights.

Rolls-Royce stopped producing narrowbody engines more than a decade ago. Embarrassingly, this meant the company completely missed a boom in demand for best-selling aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320.

But the company’s UltraFan demonstrator offers a route back in, thanks to the engine’s scalability.

Under the leadership of Tufan Erginbilgic, the company is preparing to spend £3bn on developing a narrowbody UltraFan demonstrator.

Rolls is understood to be seeking hundreds of millions of pounds in subsidies for the programme and has raised the issue at the highest levels of Government.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, have visited the company’s Derby headquarters multiple times and used its factory as a backdrop for major announcements.

UltraFan could be a game-changer for several reasons, including improving overall efficiency by around 10pc compared to today’s engines. Engineers believe the design can be scaled up or down, meaning it could be used to power both narrow and so-called “wide-body” aircraft, which have two aisles between the seats.

Insiders acknowledge that the technology improvements also have clear potential applications in defence, such as increasing the capacity or flight time of air tankers, or enabling more compact fighter jet engines.

At a time of global instability and questions about the reliability of the US, such sovereign capabilities are seen as increasingly important.

Still, Germany has already played a crucial role in the development of the first UltraFan demonstrator.

Dahlewitz engineers co-created the power gearbox in a joint venture with domestic company Aerospace Transmission Technologies.

At the same time, Dahlewitz is where Rolls develops and manufactures the Pearl range of business jet engines and is developing new, even smaller engines for “loyal wingman” drones.

However, state and federal government officials are urging Rolls to consider assigning the German site a role in other parts of UltraFan as well.

That would be controversial in Britain, with sites such as Derby currently responsible for designing and developing sections, including the fans, core and combustion chamber.

Within these specialisms lie some of the most valuable and closely guarded secrets in the British aerospace industry.

Jobs in this field are also high-value and distributed geographically across the UK, with any loss of production to foreign factories likely to be politically controversial.

“These jobs are a relatively small component of overall employment, but they are disproportionately high productivity,” Bismarck’s Whitton adds.

“They are important to exports and research and development spending, so they should arguably be prioritised over measures that just increase employment more generally.”

‘Aviation is becoming an important offset’

But Germany is equally desperate to pull them in, as its traditional strongholds are increasingly threatened by a flood of cheap goods from China, says Sander Tordoir, chief economist at the Centre for European Reform.

“German chemicals and carmaking, which is still Germany’s most important industry, are really in deep trouble,” he says.

“So aviation is becoming an important offset – it’s a sector that’s actually growing.”

According to the German Aerospace Industries Association, the number of aerospace jobs rose to 120,000 in 2024, up from around 105,000 a decade earlier.

The country’s aviation industry has long been anchored by Airbus, which conducts final assembly of some aircraft there and also manufactures fuselage sections, cabins, and wing parts domestically.

Germany is also home to Rolls-Royce’s power systems business, which manufactures diesel and gas generators, as well as engines for ships and trains, among other things.

But the country currently lacks a champion capable of making complete commercial jet engines – a weakness that has left it exposed in a row with France over who will get to make what for a next-generation warplane.

This might also partially explain why German officials are so keen to build up this particular muscle, along with the threat from China and the high value of aviation jobs, says Tordoir.

“Airbus is a German-British-French-Spanish collaboration and has done very well. But of course, that’s not how politicians will think of it in the short run,” he adds.

Certainly, some alarm bells should be ringing in Whitehall, according to Bismarck’s Whitton.

But he questions whether ministers and their officials are prepared to act, despite Rolls having recently secured substantial work funded by UK taxpayers.

Rolls was recently chosen by the Government to design the country’s first mini nuclear power plants and has separately won orders for specialist military reactors that will be needed for the Royal Navy’s Dreadnought and Aukus-class submarines.

But in some ways, the fact that Rolls is the sole supplier of some of these technologies gives the Government less leverage in the dispute, not more.

There is also little cash available for handouts, given the constrained state of public finances, whereas in Germany, the government is committing significant spending to defence and infrastructure.

“I think in France, this just wouldn’t happen,” says Whitton. “In part because [national aviation champion] Dassault is family-run and has significant state ownership.

“But unfortunately, Whitehall does not have a tremendously strong hand to play here.

“We talk about Rolls-Royce as a British crown jewel, but that’s got to be heavily caveated. Today it is a fairly globalised, export-led, publicly traded company that does not have a primary loyalty to the British state.”

Still, he argues ministers should fight hard to keep these jobs here.

“They need to have a tough conversation with them,” he says.

The Government has so far attempted to dismiss such concerns. A spokesman said the UK has one of the most competitive aerospace sectors in the world and that it values “the role Rolls-Royce continues to play, supporting thousands of high-quality, high-wage jobs”.

Yet with Germany on Britain’s tail, ministers may soon wish they had paid more attention.

A Rolls-Royce spokesman said the company had a “long-standing relationship with the State of Brandenburg” and looked forward to “building on our successful collaboration in the future”.

data.title: Revealed: how much a nearby new estate really knocks down your house price
data.description:
Do you own a home near a new development? We want to hear about your experience, good or bad. Email money@telegraph.co.uk*

So-called “Nimbys” have become a scapegoat, accused of standing in the way of Labour’s vision to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

But a Telegraph investigation reveals that they do so with good reason: new housing developments really do have a detrimental effect on the values of existing homes in the area.

House prices in the English countryside fell by £7,000 on average after major developments were built within half a mile, analysis shows, compared with a £3,000 rise for other homes in the area.

Rural homes within eyeshot of new developments decreased in value by 2.7pc over the past four years. By contrast, house prices in the same towns but further away grew by 1.3pc.

It comes as the Government introduces measures to speed up building in an attempt to meet its housing targets.

The homes that take a hit

The majority of Britons support, in principle, a large increase in the amount of new housing, according to a YouGov poll which found that 52pc were in favour compared with 37pc who were not.

However, when it comes to house building in their local area, that support turns to opposition.

In densely packed cities, residents generally understand that new housing projects bring necessary urban renewal. An ageing estate in poor condition may be torn down to make way for a modern development, or a disused industrial site turned into an affordable housing hub.

But it’s a different story in the countryside.

Opponents of building projects – often referred to as Nimbys, which stands for “not in my backyard” – believe that they spoil the British countryside, disturb the bucolic quiet, damage existing homeowners’ views or quality of life and put strain on local services and amenities. They argue that their property prices will fall if their rural homes become less desirable because of nearby modern developments and busier local populations.

The Telegraph’s analysis, using official transaction data from HM Land Registry, looked at new-build sales in villages and towns with a population of below 20,000.

It included only areas with a minimum of 50 new housing transactions in 2024 that were within a quarter-mile radius of each other to ensure that the housing project was significant enough to change the local landscape. The same threshold was applied to the number of established home sales in the neighbourhood.

There were 14 areas that met these criteria, covering the breadth of England from Essex to Yorkshire to Northumberland.

In 10 of those areas, house price growth was weaker near the new-build homes compared with the rest of the area. In nine of those 10 areas, homes near new estates lost value.

Skelmanthorpe, six miles outside the Peak District National Park in West Yorkshire, is the most extreme example of the trend.

In 2024, Land Registry transaction records indicated a cluster of 53 new-build sales within a quarter-mile radius. On a former patch of grassland on the town’s west side, the new suburb of Weavers Place had sprouted.

In the two years before this, existing detached and semi-detached homes within half a mile of the new development sold for an average of £361,000. In the two years after, this had plunged 18.5pc to £295,000.

Slightly further out in Skelmanthorpe – between half and three quarters of a mile from the site, and so with access to all the same services, bus connections and green space – prices rose by 5.9pc over the same period.

Nigel Bishop, of buying agency Recoco Property Search, said: “The majority of buyers wanting to purchase a home in the countryside seek quiet and natural surroundings.

“A new-built complex in eyeshot of their desired property would very much disturb the peace and aesthetics associated with country living, making the property less desirable to house hunters.”

He also explained that potential buyers could be concerned about additional developments being built in the future.

He said: “House hunters would then also be concerned about the area’s general infrastructure and how such developments impact the existing drainage system, Wi-Fi signal and whether the roads can accommodate increased traffic.

“The fear of the unknown and what is yet to come will cause buyers to walk away.”

Skelmanthorpe may be the most extreme example from the dataset, but it is by no means the only countryside location where a new development hurt local house prices.

In 2021, construction began on a new development of 254 homes called Bingham Gate in the market town of Bingham, Nottinghamshire. Since the project was completed, homes within half a mile of Bingham Gate have decreased in value by 10.1pc, from £379,058 to £340,787. Meanwhile, properties further away increased in value by 0.7pc to £362,525.

After Yew Tree Park came to Burscough, West Lancs, house prices nearby dropped by 3.2pc, whereas homes more than half a mile away increased in value by 4.5pc.

It is a similar story in Bourne Springs, a development in South Kesteven, Lincs, where local house prices fell by 6.9pc, and Whittlesey Park in Fenland, Cambs, where existing property values dropped by 4.1pc. Prices also fell in the wider area but not by as much.

Labour has pledged to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament yet the Government has so far failed to meet its yearly target of 300,000.

In fact, housebuilding has continued to decline in the worst slowdown since the pandemic. Rating agency S&P Global’s housebuilding subindex fell to 33.5 in December, the lowest reading since May 2020.

Residential applications for planning permission fell 9pc during Labour’s first year in office, while completions dropped almost 13pc to their lowest 12-month tally in nearly a decade.

But the Government is introducing measures designed to accelerate housebuilding. In November the Ministry of Housing announced that housebuilding near well-connected railway stations will be given the green light by default.

Former housing secretary Angela Rayner’s hallmark Planning and Infrastructure Bill finally became law in December. In bad news for rural homeowners and their house prices, her successor Steve Reed has said this means property developers will be “getting spades in the ground faster” from now on.

data.title: The home renovation dream is dead – it’s just too expensive
data.description:
Are you a landlord looking to sell up because of Labour’s baffling policies? Email us your about your experience at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

I knew when I bought my Art Deco dream home in May 2024 that I was going to be in for a rollercoaster ride.

It hadn’t been touched for decades. I knew the renovation costs would soar. I knew the timeframes would shift. And I knew I faced significant challenges (on top of the rental properties I already own) that would make me wonder why I ever decided to take out a bigger mortgage and sign on the dotted line.

But I have a love for period property. It wasn’t listed, thankfully, and was in a dated but liveable condition. After many years of renovating for others, I realised it was time to apply my knowledge and skills.

Refurbishing a property is a particularly British quirk – especially for my generation (X as I was born in 1975). For us, buying a property to “do up” and moving up the ladder was a property owner’s rite of passage.

But what I’ve come to realise – some 25 years after buying my first property – is that home renovation is now an elitist hobby where only those with money and know-how can succeed.

I first realised the challenges when I tried to buy a decent bathroom suite. I wasn’t aiming for anything extravagant, but I wanted something better than a rental standard. A quote came in at over £5,000 for just the suite and the taps. And that was with a trade discount. It didn’t help that VAT amounted to around £1,000 of that sum.

Several variations of the quote followed: swapping out taps, vanity units, baths, and before I knew it, the bathroom design had deviated so much from my original plan that I was no longer certain I wanted it.

But then the next reality set in – this was just the cost of the basics. I still had to add the cost of the tiles, the labour for fitting and tiling, the new radiator, the plastering, the electrics, the paint and the flooring.

All-in, the bathroom budget was now well more than £12,000, and if I was being honest, probably closer to £15,000.

Then there are hidden costs, such as waste removal. Since my local recycling centre does not accept tiles or bath suites, the only option was to hire a skip at an additional cost of £450.

I know that problems always arise when doing building work, so I was prepared with a contingency budget. But what I wasn’t expecting was to learn that my 45-year-old, fully functional (but admittedly not very efficient) boiler and water cylinder were incompatible with the new, modern taps.

Four plumbers offered a variety of solutions, ranging from keeping the old system and adding a pump, to changing the entire system, replacing with either a combi, an accumulator, a pumped accumulator or a stored combination boiler.

The assortment of options on offer has bewildered me, along with the prices, which are all over £4,000.

Given I hadn’t been planning to touch the heating and hot water system (I was saving for doing that next year), the learning curve has been steep.

Even if there had been any surplus funds, I would have used them to upgrade the two 30-year-old aluminium windows in the bathroom. However, having understood that I can’t have it all, I made peace with the fact that they were double-glazed and added them to a separate wish list for the future.

Typically, when it seems a project cannot go further off track, new challenges may still arise and test your abilities. In my case, a roof leak into the bathroom ceiling that was fixed in March 2025 at great expense – scaffolding, chimney repointing and new tiles quickly added up to thousands – returned two weeks before my project began.

The original roofer denied responsibility and refused to become further involved.

So now I wait for another roofer, a new start date, and search for spare change to cover the next bill.

At this point, I completely understand why councils are selling off properties that need updating. The economics are insane.

For me, I’ve had to reignite my love of DIY. It is the only cost-effective way I can get things done.

Although I am skilled with tools, I am surprised by the rising cost of everyday essentials. I remember when masking tape was available in packs of six for a very low cost – now they are £5 each!

Paint prices are another cause for upset. Buying just two sample pots cost me £12.98 (with trade discount), so unfortunately, Farrow & Ball paint can’t be on my agenda.

I know I’ll get there in the end. And maybe, in time, I’ll say all the stress, hassle and expenditure were worth it. But what concerns me more is how the economics of home renovation has been turned on its head.

Finding a property that needed refurbishment used to be a way to buy at a discount. It used to be how you moved up the ladder. Nowadays, it has become a luxury few can afford.

data.title: Spanish train crash caused by ‘gap in track’
data.description:
Experts investigating the cause of a train crash in Spain that killed 40 people have found a broken joint on the rails.

The tail end of a train heading from Malaga to Madrid derailed at 7.45pm on Sunday, smashing into an oncoming train travelling from the capital to Huelva.

Investigators at the scene identified some wear on the joint between sections of the rail, known as a fishplate, which they said showed the fault had been there for some time, a source briefed on the investigations told Reuters.

They ‍found that the ‌faulty joint created a gap between the rail sections that widened as trains continued to travel along the track, which was renovated in May as part of a €700m (£608m) project.

Technicians believe the faulty joint is key to identifying the precise cause of the high-speed crash, the source added.

The first carriages of the train that derailed, operated by Spanish firm Iryo, drove over the gap in ‌the rails. But the eighth, ‍and last, carriage derailed, bringing with it the seventh and sixth carriages, the source said.

Authorities are yet to establish an official cause of the crash, with Oscar Puente, the Spanish transport minister, calling the collision “truly strange”.

The Iryo train was less than four years old and passed an inspection on Jan 15.

Álvaro Fernández Heredia, the president of Renfe, the operator of the second train, ruled out the possibility of human error or excessive speed.

The speed limit on the track is 250km/h (149mph), while the trains were travelling at 210km/h and 205km/h when they collided.

In the months leading up to the crash, the company that manages Spain’s railway infrastructure warned of at least eight technical incidents on the high-speed line.

Most of the problems related to the signalling system, although there were also issues related to overhead power lines and other infrastructure.

The government was asked about the issues last summer and responded by saying it had addressed two “technical incidents affecting the signalling systems”.

In a statement, Iryo said it “deeply regretted what had happened” and that it had activated “all its emergency protocols”.

Authorities said there were 20 seconds between the Iryo train veering off the track and the subsequent collision, leaving the driver of the second train – who was killed in the crash – with almost no time to react before the impact.

Mr Heredia said the signalling and track-based safety mechanisms designed to detect an occupied track had no time to activate.

He also warned that determining the exact cause of the crash could take several days and that the circumstances were “rare and difficult to explain”.

The investigation is being led by the Commission for the Investigation of Railway Accidents, which operates within Spain’s transport ministry.

A separate judicial investigation has also been launched by a court in Montoro, Cordoba.

Juanma Moreno, Andalucia’s regional president, said: “It is likely [that more dead will be found] when you look at the mass of metal that is there. The firefighters have done a great job, but unfortunately, when they get the heavy machinery to lift the carriages, it is probable we will find more victims.

“Here at ground zero, when you look at this mass of twisted iron, you see the violence of the impact.”

Mr Moreno said that authorities were also searching the area nearby. “The impact was so incredibly violent that we have found bodies hundreds of meters away,” he added.

Video released by Spain’s Guardia Civil showed the sides of the worst-hit carriages shredded open, with train seats lying on the surrounding gravel.

One carriage lay on its side, bent around a large concrete pillar, with debris scattered around the area.

“There were moments when we had to remove the dead to get to the living,” Francisco Carmona, firefighter chief of Cordoba, told Onda Cero radio.

Javier Mesones, a 36 -year-old painter from Adamuz, said he heard about the crash on a WhatsApp group and set off with a friend to see if they could help.

The Guardia Civil did not let them access the crash zone in their car so the pair returned with a quad bike that became an unlikely rescue vehicle.

“There were people struggling to get clear of the wreckage and lying injured, so we pulled them onto the front rack of the quad and got them up the slope to safety – about 15 people,” Mr Mesones told The Telegraph.

“It was dark, sirens were blazing but what I saw was a catastrophe, a bloodbath. I’ve never seen anything like it in my life,” he added.

As well as the pain from wounds and injuries, passengers suffered from cold and shock on a night where the temperatures at the crash area dropped as low as 2C.

Agustín Pérez, another Adamuz resident, broke open a fence to allow vehicles access to a track that runs parallel to the railway to grant access to ambulances and rescue teams.

The 44-year-old owner of a wildlife tourism company said: “The thing that hit me hardest were the screams of pain from the carriages.”

Amid the chaos, Guardia Civil rescue workers found a six-year-old girl wandering alone with barely a few scratches on her head.

On Monday it emerged that the rest of the girl’s four family members who were travelling home to Punta Umbría in Huelva had died.

A sports centre in Adamuz was turned into a makeshift hospital, and the Spanish Red Cross established a help centre for emergency service workers and people seeking information.

“The scene was horrific. It was terrible,” Rafael Moreno, the mayor of Adamuz, told reporters. “People asking and begging for help. Those leaving the wreckage. Images that will always stay in my mind.”

Spanish King Felipe VI expressed his condolences, adding that the royal house was looking to visit Adamuz in the coming days.

“I understand the desperation of the families and the number of injured people who have suffered this accident, and we are all really worried,” he said.

On Monday, the Spanish flag was flown at half-mast in front of Madrid’s Parliament, while Pedro Sánchez, the country’s prime minister, visited the crash site.

Spain has spent decades investing in high-speed trains and currently has the largest rail network in Europe for trains over 155mph, with more than 1,900 miles of track, according to the European Union.

Sunday’s crash was the first with deaths on a high-speed train since Spain’s high-speed rail network opened its first line in 1992.

data.title: Overworked staff to blame for ghost train fiasco, says regulator
data.description:
Overworked staff were to blame for a timetabling blunder that left empty “ghost trains” running from Manchester to London, the head of Britain’s rail regulator said.

John Larkinson, the chief executive of the Office of Rail and Road (ORR), told MPs that the department was too “stretched” by its workload to consider every application to run trains.

This led the ORR to refuse Avanti West Coast permission to operate a 7am passenger service from Manchester Piccadilly to London Euston – even though the train had to leave at that time to be available for a 9.30am departure from the capital to Glasgow.

The decision meant that a fully crewed, 600-seat Pendolino train stood at Piccadilly with its doors locked before heading south in full view of passengers, who were left to wait before cramming on to later services.

Uproar over the “ghost trains” led Peter Hendy, the rail minister, and Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, to intervene to force Mr Larkinson to reinstate the 7am service – with Ms Alexander saying that “passengers were rightly baffled” by the ORR’s original decision.

However, in a letter to the Commons transport select committee, Mr Larkinson pointed to a clash between the ORR and Network Rail as an underlying cause of the fiasco.

Growing tensions

ORR staff had inherited a huge workload of timetable changes dumped on it by the track operator, which refused to carry out its usual role of allocating train routes, he claimed.

Since the ORR lacked the specialist resources of the far larger Network Rail, he wrote, “the whole process was running late,” leaving the regulator – which would normally act only as a final arbiter – “stretched and trying to close out multiple interacting decisions”.

He told MPs: “Network Rail’s approach to recent applications has been to decline to agree any new access contracts, even if it supports them.

“As a consequence we were working through 82 complex and competing track access applications relating to the December 2025 timetable, within constrained timescales.

“Acting as the first-instance decision-maker, rather than as a reviewer, placed greater demands than usual on us.”

The comments hint at growing tensions within the railway industry, as different bodies jostle for position before nationalisation.

Network Rail will be merged with train operators to create the new Great British Railways, while the ORR is expected to see its powers diminished, occupying more of an advisory role.

Mr Larkinson, who is leaving the ORR after 20 years in April, said the ORR had initially chosen to block the 7am train to create a “firebreak” between services that would help cope with any delays to crucial morning services.

He admitted that the decision was a mistake but said staff had understood that the empty train would run to London from its depot without a crew and fitting in between other services.

While Avanti later notified the ORR that the train would in fact run empty from Piccadilly, this was not “escalated appropriately”, he said.

A spokesman for Network Rail said it provided detailed recommendations on all 82 route applications that were before the ORR, including the Manchester departure, which it had proposed should be approved

data.title: The £11bn rail upgrade that will take 30 years to finish
data.description:
When Labour excitedly announced “the biggest transformation to travel in the North in a generation”, you’d have forgiven rail commuters for asking: “Which generation?” 

For all the government’s trumpeting last week of Northern Powerhouse Rail (NPR), and of £45bn to be allocated to railway station improvements, new routes and upgraded lines, it didn’t take much unpicking to notice that some of the work won’t even start for another 20 years. No new track is likely to be laid this decade. Most of today’s commuters will probably have retired by the time these schemes finally welcome trains. If indeed they ever do.

“If you look at what was actually committed to in the announcement,” sighs transport commentator Christian Wolmar, “there’s some great ideas, but the first £1bn set aside is in yet more business cases and planning for Northern Powerhouse Rail – a scheme that was first mooted by George Osborne in 2013.

“Another £1bn before a spade even goes in the ground. It’s just insane.”

Meanwhile, Westminster sources tell us that even though local mayors such as Greater Manchester’s Andy Burnham, West Yorkshire’s Tracy Brabin and South Yorkshire’s Oliver Coppard have publicly supported the plans after pushing for NPR for some time, it’s an incredibly fragile peace.

As one MP with a transport portfolio admits: “If it ends up being an expensive plan that doesn’t deliver a single mile of railway, then there’ll quickly be dissension in the ranks.” 

It was interesting that at Wednesday’s announcement, Rachel Reeves promised that lessons had been learnt from past rail infrastructure issues, such as HS2. The ongoing £11bn Transpennine Route Upgrade (TRU) scheme was cited as an example of a rail project which is proceeding on time and on budget.

And as the TRU’s managing director, James Richardson, stares up at a welder preparing the steelwork for a massive new £60m railway viaduct crossing the Calder and Hebble Navigation waterway in Ravensthorpe, West Yorkshire, he is keen ram home the message that this time things will be different. 

“It’s incredible work we’re doing,” says Richardson of the scheme to improve railway lines and infrastructure between York and Manchester.

“We need to deliver today for the capability of tomorrow.

“Tomorrow” is, though, the nebulous term here. As work on the TRU ticked over into 2026, it marked a staggering 15 years until it is due to be completed. If you take the railway upgrade plan of 2011 as the TRU’s first green signal light, we’re only halfway through what you’d forgive the people who use this line, calling an interminable 30-year process to improve just 70 miles of track; the full completion date is currently 2041.

Still, some TRU projects have now been completed – although even then, it took 10 years from the initial planning to major works beginning in 2021. And even now, with multiple sites in play, there’s still bureaucracy to navigate: late last year, it was announced that upgrading just seven miles of track between Stalybridge and Diggle would cost £225m – and that the Government had committed to funding the design work of this stage, but not yet its delivery.

Network Rail – which is ultimately responsible for this project – still has to apply for Transport and Works Act orders to get these projects lined up. The general consensus is that everyone prefers to work in this way because it’s easier to compartmentalise specific improvements and stage funding. That’s effectively the way NPR is likely to operate, too.

But you can see why industry experts are suggesting that if this were London – or anywhere else in the world, for that matter – we wouldn’t even be waiting for the funding to be allocated; the improvements probably would have been finished.

“You can take almost any European country and see the difference in approach,” says Wolmar, “They build a high-speed line, realise it’s a success and then develop a delivery agency to connect their cities.

“They don’t bother with detailed business cases and fancy bridges – they standardise the approach and the design, and build a whole network of high-speed lines for a similar amount that we’re talking about for NPR”.

For the TRU at least there is some mitigation as to why it’s taking so long. This isn’t a new line, it’s an upgrade which needs to do so much more than simply make do and mend Victorian infrastructure.

Right on cue, Richardson points out a plaque on the current bridge dating back to 1847.

“So we do recognise there is a huge desire to get the upgrades in place as quickly as possible, because of the benefits it will bring,” he admits. “But we have to balance that with the fact that we have passengers going through our network every day. We’re servicing around 15 million people in the North, and we’re actually constrained more by how much access we can get to the railway to deliver the works than the construction elements themselves.

“But the benefit of this staged approach is that there won’t be a decades-long wait for some kind of big-bang opening. We can deliver phased improvements and unlock elements such as timetable changes or station developments as we go.”

To that end, the TRU is keen to highlight that a quarter of the route is already electrified, and it’ll be up to 40 per cent by the summer of 2027.

“The headline news is always about reduced journey times, and we will deliver that,” says Richardson. “But what I think is more important here, what will be a step change for anyone travelling across the Pennines, is that this work will soon unlock around 300 extra services a week and make everything far more reliable too.

“People will be able to have confidence that they will make that flight or that meeting. That’s our vision for TRU, anyway, that we can make a really huge change for the North of England.”

And perhaps that’s also one of the many hard-won lessons from the HS2 debacle. The intention isn’t just to make a faster railway here, Richardson argues, it’s the result of listening to commuters and regional leaders who want services to be on time, and that when they go to a newly accessible railway station, it’ll be comfortable, not just a windswept, falling-down platform. When they step on to the train, too, there should be a seat in a modern train.

That way, thinks Richardson, with community buy-in extending to using local construction workers and companies, then major, long-term infrastructure projects such as this are less likely to be at the mercy of the prevailing political winds.

All of which actually sounds promising for NPR. But we’ve been here before. By continually funding expensive strategic plans rather than delivery, projects such as Northern Powerhouse Rail will always be at the mercy of politics. Reform, for example, has in the past been against some of the major NPR projects – one of the reasons that some think Labour announced this investment as part of its much-trialled 2026 reset.

Indeed, Jerome Mayhew, the shadow rail minister, accepts that something needs to change in the way these plans are endlessly talked about by governments of every persuasion, but is not impressed that a Labour Government which promised to get major infrastructure projects moving is instead “lurching from review to review, deadline to deadline”.

Henri Murison is the chief executive of Northern Powerhouse Partnership, a business-led think tank and advocacy group focusing on connectivity, devolution and education; George Osborne is its co-president. We spoke to him before the NPR announcement, and it was telling how delighted he was by the progress the TRU was making – and how exasperated he was at the general glacial pace of change.

“TRU may have been a long time in the planning – and decisions could have been made with more haste – but it’s now into delivery, and they’re doing so on time and on budget. It’s a rare example, actually, of a successful project,” he said.

“But with Northern Powerhouse Rail, the plan has been worked on for so many years. The Government needs to stop talking about it and get on with it.

“Labour promised in opposition that this would happen, and we’ve been patiently waiting, but at some point, you’ve got to say the North of England has been underinvested in for decades – and we need this to happen now.”

So it was fascinating that at the recent announcement, Murison spoke of NPR finally unlocking potential, while still highlighting the need for improved “global connectivity across the North”. He was effectively talking about a new high-speed Manchester Airport station – but that could be decades off, if not longer.

As one rail industry insider tells us: “The idea that they’re ever going to build that line between Manchester and Liverpool is pure fantasy, because it’s not a priority.”

And will Andy Burnham get the underground station for Manchester that, when we speak to him on a busy platform at a chilly Manchester Piccadilly just before Christmas (and his current attempt to get back into Westminster politics), he’s keen to secure?

Ostensibly, we meet because he’s launching a new Bee Network train – the plan being to move Greater Manchester services run by train companies such as Northern and TransPennine Express into an integrated transport network much like Transport for London. But, face to face, you could sense the frustration in his voice that, however clearly he’s expressed a vision for not just Greater Manchester but the North in general to his Labour Party colleagues, the amount of horse-trading going on in Whitehall was proving exhausting.

“Yes, there’ve been sticking points,” he admitted. “But we cannot trade down the economic and social ambitions of Greater Manchester; we are the fastest-growing city region in the country. We deserve the same highest standard of infrastructure to support that growth.”

Were those ambitions met with the Government’s announcement? For now, perhaps Burnham is happy enough. But it was telling how guarded the rest of his statement was. There wasn’t really a green light to an underground station in Manchester, but, as he put it, “an openness” to it being developed, which the city would have to “work at pace” to prove the case for.

It may even, as part of the NPR proposals, have to fund some of the improvements itself over and above the £45bn cap, perhaps through raising business rates (as Crossrail did).

Back at Ravensthorpe, however, the work on the viaduct and the new railway station here that has been approved, costed and paid for continues. “We haven’t seen this level of investment across the North,” says the TRU’s James Richardson.

The £45bn for Northern Powerhouse Rail sounds a lot, but ultimately it still remains something of a vague aspiration until work actually begins. Where money has finally been spent, on the TRU, it’s taken too long to get to delivery.

“Well, passengers on the TRU route have already seen and will continue to see noticeable improvements very quickly,” counters Richardson. “But what we need to do now is make sure we make the right decisions so that Northern Powerhouse Rail can happen in the future.”

data.title: Bidders for bust broadband provider put off by rats’ taste for fibre
data.description:
A potential rescue bid for the bust London broadband provider G.Network has been ruled out over fears that rats have chewed through its fibre-optic cables.

Community Fibre, a rival network builder in the capital, has been considering an acquisition of G.Network from administrators, who were called in last month when its £300m of debts and broadband subscriber base of just 25,000 forced it under.

However, Community Fibre chief executive Graeme Oxby told The Telegraph the rescue would not go ahead, partly because of the cost of repairing rodent damage to cables.

“Rodents like ducts and they like fibres which are very tasty,” he said, adding the prospect of costly work to fix the damage had partly deterred a rescue of G.Network by his company.

“It’s not something we’ve been particularly interested in because we think it’s got quite a lot of structural issues and would be quite an expensive fix,” he added.

Rats are known to attack underground fibre-optic cables in search of nesting material and in some cases have been blamed for internet connections being disrupted.

Mr Oxby said his company had not carried out a technical assessment of G.Network and that there were other factors driving the decision not to bid.

Openreach blamed nesting rats for days worth of network issues in Tring two years ago, with engineers discovering rats had chewed through strong ducting, outer casing and multiple cables – which typically require a drill to penetrate.

Residents and businesses in Doncaster were also hit with rat-related broadband issues last month, with local MP and Energy Secretary Ed Miliband saying the issues were caused by “rodent damage”.

He said he had “asked [Openreach] what they’re going to do to prevent widespread outages like this in future”.

Broadband companies such as Openreach typically seal openings to cables to try to prevent rats accessing them. They can also re-route cables to avoid nests.

This is a problem for G.Network because it has laid much of its broadband cables in ducts located in the middle of busy London road, rather than under pavements like its rivals.

Industry sources said this meant the company was required to dig up the road to carry out rodent-linked repair work, leading to costly and disruptive closures.

Residents in areas of the capital including Islington have previously complained of lengthy disruption as a result of G.Network’s installation works.

G.Network was plunged into administration earlier this month just a week after it was acquired by the private equity firm FitzWalter Capital.

FitzWalter, a so-called “vulture fund” that specialises in distressed assets, will now seek to sell off G.Network’s fibre broadband network and its customer base.

The broadband firm is one of dozens of so-called “alt-net” broadband firms that have sprung up to challenge BT but found themselves under strain from surging interest costs and poor take-up.

The company was previously owned by the USS pension fund and Cube Infrastructure Managers before it fell into trouble.

Analysts have predicted a wave of mergers and collapses across the alt-net sector as startup firms struggle under the weight of their debts.

In addition to G.Network, rural broadband firm Gigaclear is poised to be taken over by lenders including Natwest and the taxpayer-backed National Wealth Fund.

But Mr Oxby played down the significance of consolidation, insisting that some alt-nets could survive to challenge BT’s dominance.

He said: “Clearly there are going to be the distressed consolidations, maybe lender-led or specialist-led, but we don’t feel that consolidation is the only answer.

“Recently it’s seemed that alt-nets only exist to consolidate. We set up to be successful competitors to the incumbent and introduce some competition into the market... I think that’s got lost a bit.”

Community Fibre grew its customer base to 429,000 last year, an increase of more than a quarter, and revenue rose by almost 50pc to £112m.

The broadband firm is owned by a consortium of investors include private equity firm Warburg Pincus and the railway pension scheme Railpen.

data.title: Gary Lineker sells stake in podcast empire to Hollywood mogul
data.description:
Gary Lineker has sold a stake in his podcasting empire to a US media group founded by Hollywood mogul Peter Chernin.

Goalhanger, the production company behind The Rest Is ... podcast series, has sold a minority stake to The Chernin Group (TCG) for an undisclosed sum.

Media analysts said Goalhanger could be worth around £100m, based on a three-fold multiple of the company’s £36m turnover last year.

The deal, which is Goalhanger’s first external investment, comes as the company looks to transform its hit podcasts into global franchises across different formats. TCG will also take a seat on Goalhanger’s board.

Goalhanger, which was founded by Mr Lineker alongside former ITV executive Tony Pastor and sports journalist Jack Davenport, last year struck a deal to turn The Rest is Football into a TV show airing on Netflix during this summer’s World Cup.

The company has also enjoyed huge success through shows such as The Rest is History, The Rest is Politics, and The Rest is Entertainment, which have built up large audiences and hosted sell-out shows in venues such as the Royal Albert Hall.

The London-based firm, which employs around 80 people, has now passed more than a quarter of a million paying subscribers, equating to around £15m in annual revenues.

Last year, it generated more than 750 million full-episode views and streams across its network.

Bosses are now targeting the US for further expansion to cash in on the growing demand for its podcasts and live shows across the Atlantic.

The production company has recently struck a number of high-profile editorial partnerships in the US, including a Rest is History series on the history of Disney and a Beatles special with the American talk-show host Conan O’Brien as a special guest.

Goalhanger is also hoping to further cash in on its intellectual property (IP) in areas such as merchandising and publishing.

Los Angeles-based TCG is a media-focused investment firm that has backed start-ups including Barstool Sports, The Athletic and Reese Witherspoon’s production company Hello Sunshine.

It is also a key investor in Substack, the San Francisco-based newsletter publishing platform that has built up 50 million active users.

It was co-founded by Peter Chernin, a former Murdoch executive who runs The North Road, the production company behind Love is Blind and New Girl.

Mr Chernin has previously been linked to potential takeover bids for ITV Studios and All3Media, the British production giant behind The Traitors.

Following TCG’s investment, Goalhanger co-founders Tony Pastor, Jack Davenport and Gary Lineker said: “Since we first met Peter and the TCG team more than a year ago, it has become increasingly clear that they share our vision for what Goalhanger can become, and are the perfect partner to help us realise that ambition.

“This allows us to accelerate our US expansion, develop new IP from our hit shows, and take our ideas into new formats, platforms and markets. With a record number of paying members, we’re building a next-generation media company rooted in trust, creativity and curiosity and this partnership marks an exciting new chapter in that journey.”

Greg Bettinelli, partner at TCG, added: “We are thrilled to be able to work with Tony, Jack and Gary as well as their amazingly talented team. Goalhanger is truly one of the world’s most compelling emerging media businesses and we can’t wait for the rest of their historical journey.”
