data.title: Santander to close 44 branches risking hundreds of jobs
data.description:
Santander has put hundreds of jobs at risk after unveiling plans to close 44 branches across the country.

The lender, which recently acquired high street rival TSB, could cull nearly 300 jobs as part of the cost-cutting plan, which is in response to more customers using digital banking.

It means the company will be left with just 305 sites across the country – with only 80pc of those offering vital services such as mortgage advice as well as in-person cash and cheque deposits.

Most of the closures will be carried out in April and May this year while an additional four sites are set to be completed in January 2027.

The closures are one of the final moves by Mike Regnier, Santander’s UK chief, who is stepping down before March after five years at the helm.

Santander is owned by Spanish giant Banco Santander and was formed through a series of mergers involving Abbey National, Bradford & Bingley and Alliance & Leicester.

Banks have been retreating aggressively from the high street in recent years, with Barclays, Lloyds, NatWest and HSBC also closing branches.

However, customers and small businesses who rely on cash fear the retrenchment means they will no longer be able to make deposits.

In response, banks have launched shared sites known as banking hubs to offer deposit services, but many are still yet to open.

Santander’s fresh closures come after it unveiled plans to shut down 95 branches last year, putting 750 jobs at risk.

The closures follow Santander’s decision to recommit to Britain after reports that it planned to exit the country over excessive red tape.

Low returns from Santander’s UK business because of stringent rules introduced in the wake of the financial crash had led to rumours that it was considering selling its British business.

The bank was also forced to set aside £295m to cover costs stemming from the UK’s car finance scandal.

In October last year, Mr Regnier even urged Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to intervene in the case, warning that it risked damaging the UK economy.

However, in July, Santander struck a £2.6bn deal to buy TSB from Sabadell in a major expansion of its footprint in the country.

The takeover is set to see five million TSB customers transferred over to the banking giant. It will also take ownership of all 218 of the Edinburgh bank’s branches across Britain.

A Santander spokesman said: “In response to a continuing and sizeable shift towards customers using digital banking, we are making changes to our branches to better support our customers.”

data.title: Octopus Energy to sell power in China
data.description:
Britain’s biggest energy supplier Octopus has reached a deal to supply power in China.

Under the deal, announced during the Prime Minister’s official visit to Beijing, Octopus Energy will partner with PCG Power, a Chinese state-backed utility, to trade in China’s renewable energy market.

The partnership reflects Britain’s push to export energy technology overseas. Octopus will contribute its energy trading software to the venture, while PCG Power will provide local expertise on China’s 2.65 terawatt renewable energy market.

The deal comes as Sir Keir Starmer’s attempts to improve ties with China, despite concerns over human rights issues and in spite of US president Donald Trump’s warning against closer links with Beijing.

The joint venture, Bitong Energy, is expected to generate about £50m in annual profits by 2030, with half of those flowing back into Britain. 

It aims to trade up to 140 terawatt hours of renewable power annually by 2030 and is targeting a valuation exceeding £500m within five years.

Greg Jackson, founder and chief executive of Octopus Energy, said: “China’s investments in scale and innovation have made solar, wind energy and batteries cheaper.

“Now there’s a huge opportunity for Britain to succeed as we build the solutions that use these products to cut the cost of electricity.”

He added: “Our partnership with PCG aims to export our world-class energy capabilities to grow our economies and speed up the shift to cheaper, cleaner and more secure energy.”

The deal is likely to amplify the debate over China’s growing role in the UK’s energy industry and its links with key British power companies.

China dominates global supply chains for hardware such as wind turbines and solar panels. The UK’s reliance on China for net zero technology has raised concerns about over reliance and fears about cyber security risks.

Ministers also have yet to decide whether Shanghai-listed turbine manufacturer Ming Yang, which has partnerships with both Octopus and PCG Power, will be allowed to build a wind turbine factory in Scotland.

Octopus has said it wants to deploy six gigawatts-worth of Ming Yang turbines into onshore wind farms around the UK.

Zoisa North-Bond, chief executive of Octopus Energy’s power generation arm, has called on Britain to embrace Chinese technology such as wind turbines and solar panels.

“We look to China and we see the sheer innovation and speed at which they have deployed wind models and how much they have invested in innovation — and you cannot ignore that,” she told the FT earlier this week.

data.title: Tourism chief: ‘Tick-box’ ban on mini toiletries is green madness
data.description:
Tourism chiefs are fed up with net zero zealots forcing them into ludicrous “tick-box” green initiatives such as banning mini shampoo bottles from hotel rooms, a billionaire cruise boss has warned.

Manfredi Lefebvre, chairman of the World Travel and Tourism Council (WTTC), said travel companies around the world will no longer be bounced into measures like outlawing mini toiletries just to prove their eco-credentials.

The Italian businessman said there was a growing industry backlash, especially in parts of Asia, against campaigns which pay lip service to protecting the environment.

Lefebvre, who took over the WTTC last September, said the group’s own 2021 campaign against single-use plastics in hotel rooms encapsulated the wider problem of the industry being captured by eco-warriors.

“We have to focus on real measures, not fake measures that just tick a box,” he said.

“We don’t have shampoo bottles any more, you have the thing attached to the wall. Why is that? It’s more important to stop polluting the ocean than worry about plastic bottles in a hotel room which were mostly refilled anyway.

“Ships don’t throw things out because they will be fined but the amount of waste that comes down the rivers is huge.”

He said the net zero agenda was driven by the “priorities of people in Europe” which were “different from the priorities in China, India and Indonesia”.

“This entire environmental agenda is very much a Western one. That doesn’t mean that it’s not relevant. It is relevant but it’s not the only thing,” he said. “Problems have to be tackled in a way that is effective and not ideological.”

The EU will formally ban the provision of shampoo, shower gel and lotion bottles smaller than 100ml in hotels from 2030 as part of a wider drive to cut packaging waste.

Lefebvre, 72, knows a thing or two about tourism. He once owned Silversea Cruises, which he inherited from his father before selling it to Royal Caribbean.

Now worth more than $2.3bn (£1.7bn), his main venture today is the Monaco-based Heritage Group, which owns Crystal Cruises and the luxury travel firm Abercrombie and Kent.

Since taking over the WTTC last year, the cigar-chomping chief has been something of a hurricane blowing through the organisation.

Lefebvre’s attempt to focus on issues rather than ideologies was one of the factors behind a controversial decision last year to relocate the WTTC’s headquarters from London to Madrid, with the loss of dozens of UK jobs.

The WTTC was founded in London in 1990 by industry bosses including Bill Marriott, the hotel entrepreneur, as well as Colin Marshall, the British Airways (BA) boss, and James Robinson, the chief executive of American Express.

This left it with a biased Anglo-American view of the tourist industry, which meant it was increasingly out of tune with its non-Western members and their booming hotel industry, Lefebvre claims.

“One of the issues that limited the capability of the WTTC was that it lost contact with its member base and the evolution of the travel industry,” he said.

“It was so much entrenched in an Anglo-Saxon, British perception of the world that it lost track of its main purpose, which is its members and who they are and what they want.”

He said that the organisation’s agenda had become dominated by concerns, many environmental, that were often out of kilter with those of many members – leading to some exits from the trade body.

“We had been affected by a number of resignations which we were worried about. Every time it was the same message: you’re doing something that is not relevant to us. Of course, the environment is very important – but that’s not our main problem,” he said. “From the point of view of our members, issues in Barcelona and Bali have the same relevance.

“They are focused on all the world and want an organisation that can focus on all the world. Hotel chains open up one hotel every day in China – they don’t do that in the UK.”

In a sign of regime change, Julia Simpson – a former BA executive and adviser to Tony Blair – was replaced by Gloria Guevara, the former tourism secretary of Mexico, as WTTC president and chief executive.

He said the decision to move the WTTC out of London had been “painful” given his links to the UK.

“I visit England all the time,” he said. “I have a flat here. I had English nannies and as a young boy my parents would always send me here every August.”

However, he said that Spain – the world’s second biggest travel destination after France – is more plugged in to the concerns of the industry than Britain.

Lefebvre said: “I realise that for certain people the WTTC was perceived to be a British or Anglo-American institution.

“And London at the time was a very efficient hub for global business. Visas were easy and there was a large community of non-British people and the balance of costs and quality was very much in the favour of London.

“But for a number of reasons, England positioned itself out of the competitive market for many businesses, including the travel business.”

Lefebvre said Brexit had been a significant factor in the decision to leave London, with the split from the EU distancing the WTTC from France, Greece, Italy and Spain – four of the top 10 tourist destinations – and limiting its ability to recruit from a talent pool of 450 million people.

However, he said the punishing costs of being based in London had also become a major issue for an organisation which relies on its members for funding.

Operating out of Madrid will be about 30pc cheaper, he said, allowing the WTTC to spend more on marketing, studies and research, to the benefit of the sector.

Overseas employees will benefit from a 24pc flat tax and a cost of living that’s 60pc lower, he said – and they “won’t have to commute for an hour to have an affordable house”.

“Spain is very serious about the travel industry and they really cared about having us in Madrid. If I go tomorrow and meet the minister of tourism, they’ll be very happy,” he said.

“In the UK, nobody cares. It has been detaching itself from a number of global areas where it was relevant and becoming more and more irrelevant: one of them is travel.”

Far more pressing for hotel chains, tour operators and airlines, he said, are the challenges of accommodating growth as a fast-expanding global middle-class embraces travel.

That might require the WTTC to focus on strategies such as flattening out summer peaks by encouraging people to holiday in quieter periods, promoting low footfall but high potential destinations such as southern Italy or campaigning for looser visa restrictions, he said.

Unbridled enthusiasm for travel and tourism was on show in Rome in September at the WTTC’s annual global summit, he said.

Giorgia Meloni, the Italian prime minister, praised the industry as “an extraordinary generator of wealth and wellbeing”.

Lefebvre said he could not imagine the British premier speaking with such gusto had the event been held in London, or even agreeing to appear at all.

“You would probably not have Mr Starmer,” he said. “He’s interested in other stuff. The green agenda.”

data.title: Asda underpaid 53,000 staff after IT glitch
data.description:
Asda has admitted that 53,000 staff were underpaid by the supermarket after its payroll systems were damaged by a botched IT upgrade.

Allan Leighton, the supermarket’s executive chairman, said the company had reviewed its systems and discovered errors in how it calculated workers’ holiday and sick pay in the wake of an overhaul dubbed “Project Future IT”.

He said this meant 53,000 workers in total – including 33,000 people who still work for Asda – were underpaid between February 2024 and May 2025.

Asda vowed to repay workers what they were owed, along with interest payments.

Mr Leighton said: “Today, most importantly, we are putting it right for those colleagues affected, with interest.”

The admission comes after The Telegraph revealed in March 2024 that thousands of staff had received incorrect payslips following the £1bn IT upgrade project.

At the time, the company had said it was taking “immediate and proactive steps to correct this – to help ensure there will be no shortfall in pay for these colleagues this month”.

The payroll problems followed years of struggles after Asda sought to disentangle its systems away from former owner Walmart.

The upgrade was mired with setbacks, including delays and glitches. One issue affected thousands of orders for its George clothing range.

Asda only completed the update in September last year, having initially been aiming for a February finish.

In the company’s latest trading update, it blamed the IT upgrade for driving its latest sales slump.

In November, Mr Leighton said Asda’s poorer performance was “all to do with Future”. The supermarket posted a 2.8pc sales decline for the three months to the end of September.

Mr Leighton said the process to switch onto its own IT systems had been “suboptimal, to say the least”, pointing to poor integration and insufficient testing.

He said the issues set the company back in its turnaround efforts by six months.

The Telegraph also previously revealed that the botched IT work and subsequent payroll chaos had led Asda to claw back overpayments from workers.

On Thursday, Asda said it would be returning that money to staff “so that we are treating all colleagues equally”.

Of the staff who were underpaid, more than 80pc are owed less than £19 in total.

James Goodman, the chief people officer at Asda, said: “No Asda colleague will be worse off financially. Whether you currently work with us or have worked with us, we are paying back every penny owed with 12pc interest.

“Any colleague who has been overpaid will be allowed to keep the money and we are repaying the money that was recovered in March 2024 because of issues caused by the switchover to Project Future.”

He added: “We’ve also taken the steps to ensure nothing like this can happen again, validated by external experts.”

data.title: Billionaire Revolut founder switches residence from Dubai back to UK
data.description:
The billionaire founder of Revolut has switched his residency back to Britain after appearing to have moved to Dubai.

Nikolay Storonsky’s family office has filed new corporate records to disclose that he is a resident in the UK, not the United Arab Emirates (UAE), as an earlier document had stated.

The previous filing, published in October and suggesting that Mr Storonsky had moved to the UAE, was seen as a huge blow to Rachel Reeves and the latest in a billionaire exodus from Britain.

It also blindsided financial officials weighing whether to grant Revolut a full banking licence, prompting them to demand an explanation from the $75bn (£55bn) fintech company.

Mr Storonsky has said he spends some time in Dubai but does not live there permanently. He has consistently been listed as a UK resident in Revolut’s corporate filings.

The 41-year-old, worth £10bn, according to Bloomberg’s Billionaires Index, has been a frequent critic of regulators and the London stock market.

Storonsky Family Ltd, his family office, updated Mr Storonsky’s details on Oct 7 to change his stated country of residence from the UK to the UAE.

The filing prompted a string of media reports and left regulators dumbfounded, who are assessing whether Revolut should be granted a long-awaited licence that would allow it to issue loans and offer protected customer deposits.

Officials at the Bank of England and the Financial Conduct Authority reportedly sought answers from Revolut about why they had not been informed of the move.

On the same day as the filing, Mr Storonsky’s office issued an updated document, described as being used to correct “trivial inaccuracies”. However, the document was not published until this month.

Revolut currently holds a banking licence on a provisional basis, known as a “mobilisation phase”, which limits lending and deposit-taking. The process typically takes no more than a year, but the company has been in the mobilisation phase for 18 months.

The company first applied for a banking licence more than five years ago.

A Revolut spokesman said: “Revolut operates across 40 markets, and our CEO, Nikolay Storonsky, divides his time across the UK and our key international regions, reflecting the global nature of the business.

“There has been no change to his role or responsibilities at Revolut, and his registered details at Companies House remain in the UK.”

The Treasury said it did not comment on the tax affairs of individuals. The Bank of England did not comment. The FCA was contacted for comment.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘I was quoted £4k a year home insurance for my chocolate box thatch cottage’
data.description:
When public sector sales director Russell Kirby, 63, and his wife, Sarah, first saw their thatched cottage in the village of Dunkeswell in East Devon, they couldn’t contain their excitement.

They had been living in a detached 1950s house in Lindford, Hampshire, where Russell hadn’t really felt at home.

All that changed when they saw the four-bed chocolate box cottage with roses around the door and a large garden, perfect for their four dogs.

After putting in their best offer of £530,000, the pair were overjoyed when it was accepted in summer 2023.

However, one thing they hadn’t factored in was the enormous cost of building insurance, which for their new Grade II-listed home was quoted – at one point – at just shy of £4,000 a year.

A survey completed in their second year of ownership in 2025 put the home’s rebuild cost (how much it would cost to reinstate if destroyed) at just over £1m, which sent their building insurance spiralling.

The same thing is happening to other thatched property owners, with some saying they feel they are being held to ransom by insurers. One owner in Devon said their premium for a three-bed semi-detached Grade II-listed home had almost tripled, from £470 in 2018 to £1,300 this year.

Thatched homes tend to have higher insurance premiums, as fire damage is often severe and rebuilding is a long, specialised process.

But homeowners have told The Telegraph that they are now concerned that traditional thatched cottages will disappear altogether as people choose to tile non-listed properties, rather than re-thatch them, to make them easier to sell.

“My wife really wanted the house; both of us fell in love with it,” says. “I’d holidayed nearby as a child, so there were a lot of happy memories linked to the area.

“This home is my children’s inheritance, but because of these sky-high insurance costs, I do worry that some of these houses could become impossible to sell.”

The Kirbys had to get building insurance to exchange contracts on the house.

It was a probate sale, and the previous owner had insured through the National Farmers Union (NFU) Mutual, which was also the couple’s insurer at the time.

Their old home had cost them about £600 a year for both buildings and contents insurance.

Russell assumed that switching to his new home would incur an increase; it was a Grade II-listed building, after all. But he was not prepared for what that increase would be.

As the exchange loomed, Russell approached the NFU Mutual for a quote and was asked about the rebuild value. He did not know, so he just told them the home’s value of £530,000.

His quote came out at £2,808 a year, and the couple was so close to completion that they had no choice but to take the NFU Mutual up on it.

There were also strict conditions: no open flames within 100m of the house, so no barbecues, garden fires or patio heaters.

Their boiler and Aga cooker had to be serviced annually, and both chimneys in use had to be swept every 12 months, adding a further cost of around £500 a year on top of insurance.

Then, two years into living in their dream home, an NFU surveyor visited the house to audit the fire extinguishers, another condition of the policy.

“They asked again about the rebuild cost, and we had to have a survey,” Russell says. “It turned out we had been massively underinsured, and the cost of rebuilding the house was actually £1,000,224.”

New calculations then saw his premiums leap to £3,998 a year.

Having the wrong rebuild estimate could put millions of other homeowners in peril.

According to Rebuild Cost Assessment, as of September 2025, only 7pc of UK properties are correctly insured, with the average underinsured home covered for just 67pc of its actual rebuild cost.

That means that if your home is completely destroyed, you may not receive enough to rebuild it, or you may receive nothing at all.

A landscape of rising costs

According to the Association of British Insurers (ABI), the annual average price of combined building and contents insurance in the second quarter of 2025 was £391.

This rises to an average of £648 for a Grade I property and £555 for a Grade II building, according to the latest available Confused.com data from the last quarter of 2024.

Those figures may already be out of date – experts say rebuild costs have shot up massively since Brexit, Covid and the war in Ukraine.

An ABI spokesperson said: “Listed buildings or properties with unique constructions often carry additional and specific risks that require tailored insurance. For example, thatched cottages can present a higher fire risk.”

According to broker Howden, post-pandemic material cost rises have now stabilised, but new environmental legislation and climbing labour costs are pushing rebuild prices up.

The construction workforce is down by over 365,000 workers compared to 2008, and a third of skilled tradespeople – particularly relevant to listed buildings – are set to retire within the next decade.

Russell says the monthly payments were not crippling to him and his wife, Sarah, 56, a personal assistant, while they were working, but the cost would have been unaffordable after retirement.

Russell eventually shopped around and secured a much more affordable quote of £1,494 a year for both buildings and contents.

Tom Chapman, of The Home Insurer, a non-standard broker that now insures the Kirbys via underwriter Aviva, says owners should consider increasing their excess, removing wood-burning stoves, increasing their chimney height and ensuring their contents are not over-insured to bring down premiums.

An NFU Mutual spokesman said: “Thatched homes present unique risks and are often made of non-standard materials, which, if needed, will require specialist repairs, which can be significantly more expensive than the standard.”

They said his insurance increased by less than 7pc between 2023 and 2025, and the latest renewal saw a larger rise, since his sums insured increased by over 80pc.

data.title: Google forced to end AI ‘exploitation’ of websites
data.description:
Google will be forced to change its search engine after its artificial intelligence (AI) technology was accused of “exploiting” websites against their will.

In a crackdown announced on Wednesday, the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said Google would have to let companies choose whether to appear in the AI summaries at the top of search results.

The US tech giant will also be barred from “manipulating” its search results by retaliating against companies that criticise it or favouring businesses that spend heavily on Google advertising. Google denies engaging in such practices.

The restrictions, which could come into force in the next year, would signal the first action under new competition laws designed to address big tech’s dominance.

The News Media Association (NMA) – which represents titles including The Times, The Guardian and The Telegraph and has been pushing for a crackdown – said Google “exploits a dominant position in search”.

Google, traditionally known for providing links to other websites, has increasingly placed AI overviews at the top of search results.

The overviews, based on information scraped from websites, are designed to answer search queries without users visiting websites and have been blamed for significant drops in people visiting the websites themselves.

The CMA said website owners were unable to opt out of their content being used for AI overviews without blocking Google – a key source of internet traffic – entirely.

Under the watchdog’s proposals, Google will have to let publishers choose whether their sites can power AI overviews.

It will also be obliged to attribute clearer information to the original source, while also requiring Google’s search rankings to be “fair and transparent”.

Exploitation and discrimination claims

Publishers have claimed that the company prioritises search results from sites with which it has commercial relationships and demotes those that criticise it in public.

“Google should adopt non-discriminatory and objective criteria to mitigate the risk that Google manipulates the ranking and presentation of search results based on irrelevant and unfair considerations,” the CMA said in a consultation document.

Google has denied the claims and the CMA said it was hard to verify the complaints.

Owen Meredith, the NMA’s chief executive, said: “Google’s current approach exploits their dominant position in search by forcing publishers to opt out of appearing in search results entirely – reducing public’s access to trusted sources of news – unless they are willing to give their content away for free to AI chatbots that are then ranked above their own journalism.”

However, he said the CMA should do more to block Google’s web crawlers from being used to train AI models.

Ron Eden, of Google, said: “We look forward to engaging in the CMA’s process and will continue discussions with website owners and other stakeholders on this topic.”

However, he warned that “any new controls need to avoid breaking search in a way that leads to a fragmented or confusing experience for people”.

Sarah Cardell, the CMA’s chief executive, said: “These targeted and proportionate actions would give UK businesses and consumers more choice and control over how they interact with Google’s search services – as well as unlocking greater opportunities for innovation across the UK tech sector and broader economy.

“They would also provide a fairer deal for content publishers, particularly news organisations, over how their content is used in Google’s AI overviews.”

The CMA is consulting on the measures over the next four weeks before making a final decision. It is then likely to take six months for the changes to come into effect.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”

data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Liverpool Street station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming east London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station would ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”

data.title: Finance director died days after falling down stairs during work call
data.description:
A corporate finance director died after tripping on stairs and banging his head while making a work call, an inquest heard.

Clive Hyman, 64, told his colleague he had hit his head on marble steps, but was “feeling fine” and did not seek medical attention.

Cambridge-educated Mr Hyman died days later after complications from the head injury.

A warning has since been issued by a coroner that people who suffer head injuries should immediately seek medical advice.

Mr Hyman worked for 20 years at accounting giant KPMG before founding his own lucrative business consultancy firm, Hyman Capital.

A keen gym-goer, runner and cricket player, Mr Hyman was described as “active and in good general health”.

An inquest was told that he tripped on the stairs while making a work call during a trip to Bucharest, Romania, on Aug 1 last year.

He “informed a colleague that he had hit his head and was feeling ‘fine’”, the inquest heard.

Mr Hyman told his wife, Rachel, 52, that he had fallen on some marble steps but did not seek medical attention.

He had previously been prescribed apixaban following heart issues but he was not aware that the medication presented a risk in relation to head traumas.

Mr Hyman flew home and he developed a sudden, serious headache and had extremely high blood pressure on the afternoon of Aug 5.

His wife called an ambulance and was told that it was a “non-emergency”.

He took some paracetamol and went to bed. His wife later found him unresponsive.

An ambulance took him to A&E and a scan revealed that he had a left-sided subdural haemorrhage. He had surgery to relieve pressure on the brain.

Further imaging showed he had an extensive stroke and he died in the early hours of Aug 10.

Mr Hyman attended the £31,000-a-year Haberdashers’ Boys’ School – which boasts alumni including Sacha Baron Cohen, Matt Lucas and Jason Isaacs.

On leaving the Hertfordshire school, he studied natural sciences at Cambridge University before taking a job at KPMG, becoming the “Big Four” accounting firm’s youngest ever partner in 1995.

He generated millions in revenue, even helping its US partners “grow its private equity transactions services business from $3m to $110m over a three-year period” before founding Hyman Capital.

‘Love of my life’

Mrs Hyman, 52, said: “Clive was a remarkable person. He got himself a scholarship to Cambridge and became at the time the youngest ever partner at KPMG – aged just 34.

“After the accident, if I had known what could have happened I would have taken him to the hospital straight away.

“He was more worried about the cuts and bruises on his legs. He had no concern about his head. I have lost the love of my life. I am just so angry and sad for him.”

Mrs Hyman said she wants to raise awareness so other families avoid head injury deaths like this.

Sarah Bourke, assistant coroner for Inner North London, said: “Having reviewed several patient information leaflets issued with apixaban, it is evident that patients are routinely advised not to take the drug if they are ‘bleeding excessively’. In addition, they are advised to seek medical advice if they are at ‘increased risk of bleeding’.

“None of the leaflets expressly addressed the steps to be taken by a patient if they sustain trauma to the head.”

She concluded cause of death to be an accident and confirmed traumatic subdural haemorrhage, atrial fibrillation and a coronary artery bypass graft.

She said people should always seek medical help after head injuries and sent her report to the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry and Medicines UK.

data.title: Why does Octopus’s tech ‘powerhouse’ need £25m of taxpayer money?
data.description:
The Government has recently taken a stake in Kraken Technologies, the software platform developed by Octopus Energy.

Kraken has secured £25m of public money, provided by the British Business Bank, which will “help the company scale up and become a UK champion”, we are told.

Yet the same press release tells us that Kraken already serves 70 million customer accounts across four continents and is a “powerhouse”. Which rather begs the question: why does it need public money at all?

For the uninitiated, Kraken is Octopus’s customer service platform, which helps manage everything from billing to complaints.

It was purpose built for the company, but has proved so effective that other businesses now use it, including E.ON and National Grid. Octopus is currently in the process of spinning the business out but will retain a significant stake.

Governments usually nationalise or invest for one of two reasons: to bail out failing firms whose collapse would be catastrophic, such as the banks in 2008, or to fund projects that cannot otherwise attract capital, either because they are very costly to develop or because they are highly speculative but potentially transformative, such as nuclear fusion.

Kraken fits neither category: it’s already scaled, already global and already profitable (despite making a loss in its most recent reporting period).

Nor is attracting capital a problem. Media reports at the end of December said that $1bn (£725bn) was raised in Kraken’s first standalone funding round, valuing the business at $8.65bn.

This demonstrates strong investor appetite, so why did it need state support? What, exactly, is the taxpayer buying?

If we are hoping for a handsome profit, then it is probably too late. The December funding round values the business at around 17 times its contracted annual revenue, which is well above its peer group.

Since the order of events implies the Government invested after the round closed, the British Business Bank’s stake will almost certainly have been taken at or above this valuation.

Kraken’s most recent financials show growth, but it reported a loss after previously earning profits. The performance indicates that it delivered revenue growth but at the cost of margin compression.

Much of the public commentary still treats Kraken as a highly profitable, high-margin software-as-a-service (SaaS) business throwing off cash, but the accounts show something closer to an enterprise platform business investing heavily and carrying real cost of growth.

Meanwhile, SaaS companies have performed poorly on the public market recently.

All of which suggests that such a high valuation may not be justified, and bodes ill for the chances of the Government stake being very profitable.

Whether you make money investing in shares depends both on the underlying performance of the business and the price you paid, and this valuation sets a high bar to secure returns.

The other theory as to why the taxpayer is backing Kraken is to encourage it to list on the London Stock Exchange.

It is expected that the company will list its shares through an initial public offering shortly. The Government is apparently keen to ensure Kraken lists in London, which has been struggling to attract new listings, rather than in the US.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, told the Financial Times that the investment was “not a bung” but acknowledged it was part of efforts to keep the company in the UK.

But a tiny bit of cash from the UK Government – which gives it around 0.04pc of the voting rights – isn’t going to change the decision either way.

Kraken announced its December funding round in dollars and speaks increasingly of growth in the US.

As it operates in a sector where US markets typically deliver higher valuations and deeper liquidity, this would tend to make a US listing more likely. So there are major outstanding questions about the rationale for the deal.

What we do know is that selling Kraken brings in cash for Octopus that can help it to meet Ofgem’s new regulatory capital requirements.

The Telegraph reported back in July that the company was potentially £1bn short of the amount it was required to hold.

With growing customer numbers, the obligation is only growing too. Octopus also returned to losses in 2025 after just two years of profitability.

Immediately after the sale of Kraken, the software company agreed to act as a guarantor to Octopus’s loan facility with NatWest.

This resembles a reverse parent company guarantee, with the stronger subsidiary underwriting the weaker parent, and can only be a temporary arrangement since it would be difficult to list Kraken with this arrangement.

As a final kicker, there are also unresolved governance questions around the Government’s stake in Kraken.

Greg Jackson, Octopus’s chief executive, sits on the Cabinet Office board and on the Industrial Strategy Advisory Council, both of which provide strategic input to the Government.

While this does not imply impropriety, the optics are uncomfortable: the State is taking an equity stake in a business being sold by a government adviser.

Public money is supposed to address market failure, but the only market failure here is Octopus’s regulatory capital position, a problem it can solve perfectly well by selling Kraken.

Kyle claims the investment is “a sign of intent in backing innovative firms”.

Yet it is not clear whether it delivers value for money for the taxpayer or any meaningful strategic benefit for the country. We need more answers on this deal.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Royal Mail and Evri risk millions in fines for losing parcels
data.description:
Royal Mail and Evri could face millions of pounds in fines as part of a crackdown on lost and delayed parcels.

Ofcom has written to Britain’s biggest parcel operators to raise concerns about widespread issues with how they handle complaints and treat disabled customers.

The regulator has summoned the companies to a meeting to discuss their performance and threatened fines worth up to 10pc of their revenues if they continue to fall short.

In the letter, Ofcom bosses wrote: “In light of these concerns, we are undertaking an additional assessment of the steps parcel operators are taking to comply with the necessary regulations.

“Where our assessment indicates that specific parcel operators are not operating in line with our existing requirements, we will consider whether enforcement action is necessary or consider whether additional regulation is needed to ensure customers are adequately protected.”

Ofcom’s research has uncovered growing public concern about the performance of parcel firms. A quarter of customers said they were dissatisfied when contacting a courier about an issue, while overall satisfaction stands at just 47pc, up only two percentage points since 2024.

The regulator’s most recent survey showed that Evri was the UK’s worst delivery company for the third year running, with more than 40pc of people saying they were unhappy with its service.

Yodel came second bottom while Royal Mail ranked in the bottom half of the table. Amazon was the best performer, followed closely by FedEx and UPS.

It is understood that all parcel companies have been contacted by Ofcom, regardless of their performance in the survey.

Evri’s revenues stood at £1.9bn in 2024, meaning the company could face a penalty of up to £190m. Royal Mail’s annual parcel revenues stand at around £4bn, meaning it could be fined as much as £400m.

However, Ofcom said its fines must be “appropriate and proportionate”, suggesting it is unlikely to hand down penalties on this scale for now.

The threat of fines will be a particular concern for Royal Mail, which is seeking to refocus on parcels following its £3.6bn takeover by Czech billionaire Daniel Kretinsky last year.

Royal Mail, whose letter business is separately regulated by Ofcom, has been fined more than £36m over the last three years after repeatedly missing targets for letter deliveries.

The regulator tightened its rules for parcel companies in 2023 after it found that some people struggled to make complaints when things went wrong. Operators must now have a simple and transparent complaints process in place.

Ofcom also introduced new requirements for the fair treatment of disabled people after it found that these customers experienced higher rates of problems.

Any failure to protect vulnerable customers would be taken particularly seriously, Ofcom warned in its letter. It pointed to a recent £24m fine handed to Virgin Media O2 for putting vulnerable customers at risk during the digital landline switchover.

The crackdown comes amid surging demand for deliveries. A record 4.2 billion parcels were sent and received across the UK last year, up 7pc on the previous year and eclipsing the pandemic peak in 2020-21.

“Where our assessment indicates that specific parcel operators are not operating in line with our existing requirements, we will consider whether enforcement action is necessary or consider whether additional regulation is needed to ensure customers are adequately protected.”

Ofcom’s research has uncovered growing public concern about the performance of parcel firms. A quarter of customers said they were dissatisfied when contacting a courier about an issue, while overall satisfaction stands at just 47pc, up only two percentage points since 2024.

The regulator’s most recent survey showed that Evri was the UK’s worst delivery company for the third year running, with more than 40pc of people saying they were unhappy with its service.

Yodel came second bottom while Royal Mail ranked in the bottom half of the table. Amazon was the best performer, followed closely by FedEx and UPS.

It is understood that all parcel companies have been contacted by Ofcom, regardless of their performance in the survey.

Evri’s revenues stood at £1.9bn in 2024, meaning the company could face a penalty of up to £190m. Royal Mail’s annual parcel revenues stand at around £4bn, meaning it could be fined as much as £400m.

However, Ofcom said its fines must be “appropriate and proportionate”, suggesting it is unlikely to hand down penalties on this scale for now.

The threat of fines will be a particular concern for Royal Mail, which is seeking to refocus on parcels following its £3.6bn takeover by Czech billionaire Daniel Kretinsky last year.

Royal Mail, whose letter business is separately regulated by Ofcom, has been fined more than £36m over the last three years after repeatedly missing targets for letter deliveries.

The regulator tightened its rules for parcel companies in 2023 after it found that some people struggled to make complaints when things went wrong. Operators must now have a simple and transparent complaints process in place.

Ofcom also introduced new requirements for the fair treatment of disabled people after it found that these customers experienced higher rates of problems.

Any failure to protect vulnerable customers would be taken particularly seriously, Ofcom warned in its letter. It pointed to a recent £24m fine handed to Virgin Media O2 for putting vulnerable customers at risk during the digital landline switchover.

The crackdown comes amid surging demand for deliveries. A record 4.2 billion parcels were sent and received across the UK last year, up 7pc on the previous year and eclipsing the pandemic peak in 2020-21.

data.title: Khan accused of misleading Londoners over affordable homes
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan has been accused of exaggerating his affordable homes push by falsely claiming to have started thousands of new developments.

New figures from data group Molior suggests the Mayor of London has been counting early-stage developments – such as buildings slated for demolition as well as blueprints for new homes – towards his total of affordable homes under construction.

Instead of 45,000 affordable homes claimed by the Greater London Authority (GLA) as being in progress, Molior said only 22,468 are actually being built in the capital.

Tim Craine, Molior’s founder, claimed the Mayor was misleading the public by inflating the numbers.

One project, Douglas Bader Park in Colindale, was counted by the GLA as under construction but Molior’s researchers found just 38 of the 392 affordable homes promised had been built.

“What the GLA did was count the whole estate and call a 12-year build programme as being ‘under construction’. There are dozens of examples like this across London,” said Mr Craine. “Its definition of a start is signing a contract.”

Susan Hall, the Conservative leader in the London Assembly, called Molior’s claims “very concerning”.

She said: “If the Mayor or his deputies have included buildings that aren’t being worked on in the list of houses they’ve provided, it’s a disgrace.”

Ms Hall added that “cherry-picked” data would “further [erode] trust in authorities amid an acute housing crisis in the capital”.

Sir Sadiq has previously been embroiled in a row over his housing tracking record.

A statistics watchdog last year ruled that the Mayor’s claim he had “delivered” 25,000 affordable homes in one year could be misinterpreted because many of the houses had only been started but not completed.

The Mayor of London’s target to build 88,000 homes a year in London plays a crucial role in the Government’s overarching ambition to deliver 1.5 million by the end of this parliament.

Sir Sadiq was given a reprieve last year when Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, cut the proportion of affordable homes that a developer must deliver as part of a project to boost housebuilding rates in the capital.

A target for London’s Affordable Homes Programme – a taxpayer funded scheme which forms part of Sir Sadiq’s home push – was also slashed by more than a fifth to between 17,800 and 19,000 to be built by March this year.

However, only 6,370 have begun under the programme, according to the GLA – meaning the Mayor needs to start work on at least 11,430 more homes before March to meet the total.

Tom Copley, the deputy mayor of London for housing, has previously attacked Mr Craine’s claims that civil servants “embellished” the figures.

Mr Copley said the allegation was “outrageous” and “completely unsubstantiated” at a GLA committee meeting in January.

A spokesman for the Mayor of London said: “GLA statistics on the starts and completions of homes by any tenure are transparent and accurate.

“The definition of starts on site for City Hall-funded affordable housing programmes is clearly set out in our funding contracts and are similar to those used by Homes England for equivalent projects.”

data.title: I was an eyewitness as the FT went woke and I couldn’t bear it
data.description:
The London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG) has decided to cancel hundreds of corporate subscriptions to the Financial Times (FT), it was reported this week, ending a longstanding relationship between two prominent City of London institutions that goes back to the naming of the FTSE 100 itself.

Apparently, the move stems from LSEG’s dissatisfaction with the FT’s shifting editorial priorities. As a former FT employee who resigned from the paper over concerns about institutional politicisation, I can understand where the LSEG is coming from.

I had been the editor of FT Alphaville, the paper’s innovative markets blog. To be clear, I still admire and respect the vast majority of my former colleagues. They do great work. I also maintain my own FT subscription. However, the publication’s DNA changed over the 13 years I worked there.

The FT used to be a place where elites exchanged unvarnished truths, while populist outlets peddled agenda-driven narratives to the masses. Or at least, so the adage went.

That is no longer the case. Today, I subscribe because the content helps me understand herd mentality in markets – what I call “beta money” flows.

The question is: how did the FT evolve from a candid elite forum where dialogue was a point of pride – the best of the British free-thinking tradition – to an increasingly timid, risk-averse rag with little appetite for publishing ideas that sit far outside the prevailing party line? What happened to the “without fear or favour” spirit that championed the interests of the investor? I have some ideas.

My hunch is that this change was driven by the evolution of its business model. In response to falling advertising revenue and digitisation, the FT slowly but surely moved from serving a comparatively narrow but powerful, largely UK-centred financial readership to chasing mass subscriptions.

To make this work, it had to seek out a more global, demographically diverse subscriber base with diverse values and agendas. Doing this effectively demanded diplomacy, not candour, in editorial lines.

When I joined the FT in 2008, it couldn’t have been more different. In fact, the publication hosted subversive experiments like Alphaville specifically to challenge assumptions and highlight blindspots in elites’ thinking.

But this tolerance for parsing uncomfortable truths began to decline as social media rose. Premium content began to leak outside the paywall, whether through illicit content-sharing or journalistic self-promotion. The process exposed finance journalists to a much wider range of feedback – not all of it positive.

In the early days, some of that pushback represented healthy, respectful debate, akin to what would have happened internally. But over time, as scale took over, things soured. Audiences unfamiliar with the underlying mechanics of markets, institutions and statecraft, where uncomfortable trade-offs and quiet concessions are often the substance of responsible decision-making, increasingly began to bully journalists in a politicised way, both on social media and in the comment sections. The outcome was a chilling effect on FT journalism and analysis, even if subconsciously.

That trend was compounded by corporations going “woke”, which led to a downturn in the luxury-lifestyle advertising that the FT previously relied on.

The other significant change brought about by the internet was the rise in the pursuit of clicks. Under my tenure, I saw directly how audience engagement teams increasingly dictated agendas: chase clicks, avoid flops, be popular. If X headline drives clicks, be sure to put it in the headline.

For a financial press that is supposed to serve investors, the result was audience capture of the worst kind. It cultivated adherence to an increasingly globalist agenda that prioritised the party line in Davos over serving UK investors.

The worst mistake of all? The FT mistook its pro-market and pro-globalist viewpoint as the righteous, “apolitical” path, completely missing the fact that this position had been politicised. For me, the most disappointing aspect of the paper was its reluctance to scrutinise or offer a compelling economic counterargument to lockdown during the Covid pandemic, instead aligning itself very firmly with the official consensus. If any paper had the authority to critique the policy, I always thought, it was the FT.

In turning inward and sidelining domestic dissent, the FT strayed from its free-thinking heritage and blunted its role as a place where uncomfortable truths could still be spoken.

The irony is that even the Chinese Communist Party understands that shielding elites from uncomfortable realities erodes strategic competence, which is why it maintains a two-track information system. The public gets simplified narratives, while those in power get unvarnished assessments.

Sadly, in the West, the only places market truths now percolate are behind extremely high paywalls on Substack or at private events.

Not everyone agrees with me. When I voiced this argument on social media, Olaf Storbeck, the FT’s Frankfurt bureau chief, dismissed my analysis as nostalgic sophistry – “everything was better in the past, even the future” – and challenged my claim that “woke” corporate shifts influenced editorial. He argued no direct link existed.

But the facts are the facts. The FT did, in fact, pivot to a digital-first, subscriber-driven model from 2013 onwards. It did prioritise international growth beyond its elite core. Internal memos did urge the use of “inclusive language” to attract women, which I, as a female finance enthusiast, always perceived as insulting.

Internal guidance did encourage the use of pronouns in sign-offs. And, most frustratingly of all, audience engagement teams increasingly provided input to morning editorial conferences while deprioritising the traditional City audience, treating them as dinosaurs.

None of this was done by stealth. In 2019, the FT ran its “Capitalism: time for a reset” campaign openly, explicitly signalling its new, politicised agenda. The campaign argued that the prevailing model of capitalism – especially the decades-long focus on maximising shareholder value – was under strain and needed a fundamental rethink.

Unfortunately, that rethinking shied away from entertaining the possibility that capitalism had been subverted by too many accommodations to non-democratic states.

If you were paying attention, the effects of all this were detectable in the quality of the comments on the FT’s website. When I first joined in 2008, feedback was sophisticated, and comments could give an edge in the market. London-based professionals were sharing pragmatic insights on crises, corporate boards and historical context. We built a community at Alphaville based on that dialogue.

By the middle of the 2010s, as comment volume surged, quality plunged. Comment sections were soon infiltrated with everything from low-effort foreign trolls to increasingly politicised subscribers who echoed whatever slogans were en vogue: “Stop platforming right-wing shills, or subscriptions will suffer.”

As the old-school market pragmatists were drowned out, so was the feedback mechanism to writers.

This mirrors the FT’s broader blindness. Non-partisan isn’t balanced; it’s often code for one-sided technocratic bias. As a contrarian – neither Left nor Right, but fighting for human-centred views – I have no political axe. I believe markets thrive on full pictures: diverse perspectives, unfiltered signals and non-politicised commentary that bends reality.

Markets, however, punish illusions. And the LSEG cutting back subscriptions is clearly a signal that the market is finally pushing back.
