data.title: Elon Musk’s X accuses China of flooding site with porn during civil unrest
data.description:
Elon Musk’s X has accused Beijing of flooding the social media site with pornography to drown out protests and dissent.

Nikita Bier, the social media platform’s head of product, claimed the Chinese government was to blame for the rise of pornographic spam in response to complaints from users.

He said: “The Chinese government floods X search results with porn whenever there is political unrest – to prevent their citizens from finding out real-time information.

“This has been a difficult problem to solve but we are aware [and] working on it.”

Mr Bier was responding to an X user who had complained that, when searching for Chinese-language posts on X, their results were filled with “spam and illicit ads”.

X, previously known as Twitter, has been banned in China since 2009 and blocked by the country’s Great Firewall.

However, the app remains available in Chinese-speaking regions including Hong Kong and Macau, which maintain distinct internet rules compared to the mainland. X is also available to Chinese speakers in Taiwan.

Xi Jinping, China’s president, has launched a purge of the country’s military leadership in recent days, including removing top general Zhang Youxia. The purge has prompted a flurry of speculation across the Chinese diaspora over the reasons for the ousting.

China has repeatedly been accused of using fake accounts to spread misinformation on social media and to target pro-democracy activists.

A 2024 report from disinformation researchers DRFLab found what appeared to be fake Chinese accounts harassing anti-CCP activists, including sending them death threats and videos of animals being killed.

Chinese spam accounts also flooded X in November 2022 to drown out digital protests about the country’s zero-Covid policies.

Ahead of Mr Musk’s takeover in 2022, Twitter documented multiple efforts to take down tens of thousands of fake accounts that it believed were being co-ordinated by China.

Since then Mr Musk has gutted X’s moderation and safety teams. However, in its latest transparency report for the second half of 2024, X said it removed 335 million accounts for suspected manipulation and spam.

The overt criticism of China by a key X executive contrasts with Mr Musk’s typically cautious approach to Beijing.

Mr Musk’s Tesla is highly reliant on China for its car production, with its single biggest car plant based in Shanghai manufacturing close to one million vehicles each year.

The world’s richest man has also spoken out against Donald Trump’s trade war on China and criticised the US president’s tariffs on Chinese electric car imports. The billionaire has said he is in favour of “no tariffs”.

Sir Keir Starmer flew to China for a state visit this week despite a warning from Mr Trump that doing business with China was “very dangerous”.

Mr Musk also spoke out last January against a ban on TikTok, the social media app launched by Beijing’s Bytedance. At the time, he said the proposed US ban “goes against freedom of speech”.

However, in a rare rebuke, he said also China’s longstanding ban on X was “unbalanced” and “needs to change”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.

data.title: Miliband’s net zero drive unleashes record number of solar installations
data.description:
Ed Miliband’s net zero blitz led to a record number of solar panels being installed across the UK last year, according to official figures.

A total of 262,000 solar installations were carried out in the UK in 2025, reflecting a 37pc increase year-on-year. This was the highest recorded total since 2011.

The bulk of the growth came from capacities of under 50 kilowatts, suggesting it was driven by households and businesses installing rooftop solar. Of the total number of installations, a total of 177,000 were at people’s homes.

But Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, has also approved a string of large solar farms since Labour came to power, aided by taxpayer-backed subsidies.

This sparked outrage among many households in rural areas, with some claiming that the green energy projects are a blight on the countryside.

However, Ben Graves, of solar installer Sunsave, said the numbers showed households were “looking for ways to cut high energy costs” following a surge in bills.

The amount spent on electricity by a typical household has risen by around 60pc to £963 per year since 2020, according to the GB Electricity Bills tracker website.

Mr Graves said: “People are recognising that generating and storing their own electricity is the most effective way to cut bills and protect against price uncertainty.

“These record installation figures prove there’s a strong consumer appetite, which will only increase when the right support is in place.”

He added: “Solar and battery systems deliver immediate bill savings whilst strengthening the UK’s energy security.”

The surge in solar installations will be seen as a boost to Mr Miliband, who has set highly ambitious clean power targets for 2030.

Today, there is about 21.6 gigawatts (GW) of solar capacity, including 6.3 GW on domestic rooftops, according to the new data.

But under Mr Miliband’s plan, the total needs to rise to between 45 and 47 GW in barely five years – an increase of at least 23.4 GW.

By comparison, just 7.7 GW were built in the past five years.

But the rollout is expected to gain momentum in the coming years after the Government approved a string of large solar farms in the countryside.

This includes vast projects in North Yorkshire and Lincolnshire, such as the Tillbridge Solar Farm in Gainsborough, which will cover 2,000 football pitches’ of prime farmland.

At the same time, Mr Miliband has announced plans to provide cheap loans or grants to households who install solar panels.

This is aimed at cutting the upfront cost of installation, which was still around £7,000 last year, according to installation data from the MCS Foundation, the industry’s accreditation body.

Mr Graves added: “The Government’s proposed loan financing scheme addresses the biggest barriers to adoption.

“With continued policy backing, we expect to see this upward trajectory continue throughout 2026.”

Michael Shanks, the energy minister, said: “Every solar panel installed helps us to take back control of our energy sovereignty and tackle the cost of living crisis.

“Solar is one of the cheapest and quickest forms of energy to build and these record results for installations are a win for those who want Britain to stand on our own two feet and bring down energy bills for good.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Self-driving taxi in London films itself running red light
data.description:
One of London’s first self-driving taxis filmed itself running a red light as it carried its company’s co-founder to Buckingham Palace to be decorated for services to AI.

Alex Kendall, chief executive of the British AI startup Wayve, shared dashcam footage on social media which showed that his car was operating autonomously and had ignored a red light on Parliament Square.

Mr Kendall, a Cambridge-trained AI scientist, had used the Wayve vehicle to drive him to an investiture last year where the Princess Royal awarded him the Order of the British Empire (OBE).

In a post on X this week, he said: “Many asked to see the autonomous drive to Buckingham Palace for my OBE,” hailing the car’s “impressive drive without intervention”.

It appeared he was unaware that his flagship autonomous vehicle had broken a traffic law during the journey.

Wayve has a partnership with taxi app Uber in which it hopes to launch road trials of its self-driving taxis this year. The so-called Level 4 technology will be fully autonomous, meaning it is approved to drive without human supervision or intervention.

The two companies said the project was the first-ever plan to launch public-road trials of fully autonomous vehicles in the UK.

Dashcam footage showed Mr Kendall’s car driving along Tottenham Court Road, then south through Soho and Trafalgar Square before reaching Buckingham Palace via Victoria Embankment and Parliament Square.

As the car negotiated heavy traffic on the south-west corner of Parliament Square, opposite the Supreme Court, it stopped in front of a white stop line.

A bus can be seen straddling the stop line and pedestrian crossing beyond it, having followed slow-moving cars in front on to the junction.

The traffic light controlling that arm of the junction can be seen changing from green to red. The Wayve car then drives forward, along with the bus.

Wayve admitted the car had run the red light, saying in a statement that its cars are “prototypes” and that Mr Kendall ought to have taken control and stopped.

A spokesman said: “All Wayve journeys are conducted with trained safety operators in the driver’s seat, monitoring the system during testing.

“In this instance, although it’s clear the vehicle crossed the white stop line while the light was green, the car continued to follow the flow of traffic while the traffic light was obscured from view.

“We acknowledge that the more appropriate action would have been to manually take over the vehicle and keep it stationary until a green light was visible again,” continued the spokesman.

“Our test vehicles are prototypes, and our software is under development, with a safety operator supervising every ride. We take this very seriously and will review our systems to ensure ongoing compliance and safety.”

Running a red light is an offence, carrying three penalty points and a fine of £100. Online guidance from the Metropolitan Police suggests drivers caught breaking this law would be fined or referred for a driver awareness course.

Other online advice from police suggests that “driving through a red light by mistake” could amount to “careless or inconsiderate driving”, an offence commonly referred to as “driving without due care and attention” with reference to the words in section 3 of the Road Traffic Act 1988.

Steve MacNamara, general secretary of the London Taxi Drivers’ Association, said the route taken by the Wayve car was not a sensible one and questioned how the technology would be regulated in the future.

“These cars are sold as ‘the future’,” said Mr MacNamara. “Having watched the video, I am astounded anyone would consider them suitable to be used to convey the public.

“The route it took was long and circuitous and put 20 minutes on the journey; any cabbie taking those routes would quickly lose their licence.

“The red-light jumping is dangerous and a three-point offence.

“London cabbies have their licences revoked at six points. Who is going to police these things and who is responsible for the points?”

The Labour Government is accelerating the pace at which trials of self-driving taxi and bus services will hit British roads.

Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, announced in June 2025 that self-driving passenger services could be used by members of the public as early as spring 2026 and customers could book trips using an app.

There will not be a requirement for a safety driver to be behind the wheel in the event of an emergency.

Self-driving trials have been taking place in the UK since January 2015 with Wayve among the early pioneers, which began operations in the UK in 2017.

Mr Kendall said last year: “Accelerating commercial self-driving pilots to 2026 positions the UK as a leading destination for the deployment of L4 self-driving technology. These early pilots will help build public trust and unlock new jobs, services, and markets”.

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Homeowners risk ‘medieval’ fees on extensions as architects lobby Labour
data.description:
Homeowners could be forced to hire architects to extend their properties under plans that critics have compared to a “medieval tithe”.

Changes being considered by Labour would make it compulsory for builders to consult a qualified architect before submitting a planning application – which is likely to inflate the price for home extensions by thousands of pounds.

At present, many homeowners get by without consulting architects on extensions with many using architectural technicians or architectural designers instead. Some draw up their own basic designs.

But a new rule would force builders to show they have retained a qualified architect, adding extra costs to everything from simple conservatory projects to much larger construction schemes.

Architects claim unqualified designers are “diluting” the standards of new building but critics say the changes will simply impose extra costs on homeowners.

Samuel Hughes, a housing expert at the Centre for Policy Studies think tank, compared the proposals to a “medieval tithe”.

He said: “The basic problem here is not whether or not architects are any good.

“It’s that under these proposals, people who currently don’t hire architects because they don’t really want the services of architects would be forced to do so.

“That is not going to improve design either because they can still perfectly well ignore everything the architect says anyway – so long as you can find an architect who is prepared to sign a piece of paper,” he added.

“And of course, there will always be some who are happy to do so.

“So this is a completely toothless measure in that sense. All it is going to mean is architects getting a little tithe from every project.

“It’s like in medieval Europe, under the feudal system, where various groups had ancient privileges and rights and people would be required to pay them off – which is essentially what’s going on here.”

Architects in Britain typically charge between 3pc and 15pc of a project’s total construction costs, although some also use fixed-fee structures, according to the HomeOwners Alliance.

The Government did not comment on the merits of the proposal but confirmed it was poised to hold a “call for evidence” on the “built environment professions” which is expected to examine the issue.

Officials at the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) have been discussing the change for more than a year, according to The Architects’ Journal, a trade publication.

‘Unnecessary risk’

Architects have broadly supported the idea, arguing that it will improve public safety.

“Without protection of function, we’re seeing design and technical coordination at critical stages being diluted by unqualified individuals, leading to poor outcomes and unnecessary risk,” one person told The Architects’ Journal.

Ministers are reportedly being lobbied by the Royal Institute of British Architects (Riba) and – unusually – the Architects Registration Board (ARB), the industry regulator, as well.

When approached by The Telegraph, the Government refused to comment on the policy itself and claimed it was wrong to suggest the ARB had supported the change.

This is despite comments by Hugh Simpson, the regulator’s chief executive, this month calling for a protection of function to be considered.

He told The Architects’ Journal: “We have said to [the Government] we think this would be an opportune time to consider a broader question about regulating the function of architectural work – namely, reserving certain critical activities to qualified professionals.”

Mr Simpson added: “We’ve said very clearly to government that in most EU member states and G7 countries, regulation goes beyond title.

“We think it is absolutely right and we’ve said so, to look at what regulation of function might look like.”

A spokesman for the MHCLG said: “The Telegraph has confused the Architects Registration Board, which has never proposed this change, with the Royal Institute of British Architects, which has.

“They are separate, independent organisations and it is not government policy to require architects to submit a planning application.”

They added, however, that the Government has committed to a call for evidence on the built environment professions and would publish details “in due course”.

Riba and the ARB have been approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: TfL forced to correct ‘dodgy’ report claiming floating bus stops are safe
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan has been forced once more to correct “dodgy” data he used to justify floating bus stops in the capital.

The Mayor of London has been accused of running a “masterclass in deception” after refusing to withdraw a “deeply flawed and whitewashed” safety review that was “riddled with errors”.

Transport for London (TfL) used the 26-page report to try to prove that “floating” bus stops – where pedestrians must cross a cycle lane to board – were safe.

However, disability campaigners say vulnerable pedestrians – including the elderly, blind people and parents with prams – are forced to “run the gauntlet” when crossing cycle paths because the majority of cyclists ignore zebra crossings.

The Bus Stop Bypass Safety Review claimed analysis of data from 121 bus stops before and after they became the floating type proved that elderly and disabled people were not deterred from using them.

However, the National Federation of the Blind UK (NFBUK) and The Telegraph have shown that at least 37 of those bus stops were the wrong type for the comparison.

Despite the Mayor’s statisticians having been given two weeks to recalculate their research, TfL has been forced to admit that yet more of the study could be inaccurate and that it cannot say how much of it is inaccurate. To the dismay of campaigners, however, TfL insists the findings will remain the same.

It is the second time Sir Sadiq’s report has had to be republished after significant errors were uncovered.

In 2024, The Telegraph revealed that the Mayor had to apologise to disability campaigners after it was proved that TfL failed to include a spate of serious pedestrian injuries when totting up casualties at the bus stops.

Now, NFBUK is reporting Sir Sadiq to the Local Government and Social Care Ombudsman and calling for the report to be withdrawn because TfL is beholden to the cycling lobby and “cannot be trusted”.

The body has also called on Will Norman, the Mayor’s walking and cycling commissioner, who championed the report, to resign.

Sarah Gayton, from the NFBUK charity, said: “Sadiq Khan and Will Norman have again failed blind and disabled people by peddling dodgy data and whitewashing serious concerns about these bus stops.

“They have conducted a masterclass in gaslighting and deception. This report is riddled with errors and has had to be republished twice. I will be writing to the ombudsman to report the Mayor and ask for this report to be withdrawn.

“Everyone – particularly the most vulnerable pedestrians – knows these bus stops fly in the face of common sense.

“Khan makes much about his father having been a bus driver, but he doesn’t care about those who rely on this public transport.”

She called for the floating bus stops to be scrapped, particularly because as few as one in 10 cyclists stop at zebra crossings to allow pedestrians to reach the bus stop.

Andrew Boff, the deputy chair of the London Assembly, said: “TfL is being extremely selective with their data. It has an agenda driven by the Mayor and the cycling lobby, which means that no matter how much these floating bus stops defy common sense, they will pursue them.”

A TfL spokesman said the report “continues to show that bus stop bypasses are low risk”, adding: “Unfortunately when looking at how usage has changed over time, the implementation dates of some of the bus stop bypasses had been recorded incorrectly and we are repeating this analysis with an updated list of bus stop bypasses.”

He added that TfL would continue to work with groups representing disabled people to “understand their experiences”, as well as work to “increase awareness among cyclists”.

Mr Norman failed to respond to a request for comment. But a spokesman for the Greater London Authority said the Mayor and his team were “committed to safe and sustainable travel … for everyone”.

He added that the report showed “very low injury risks to pedestrians as well as high levels of use by both disabled and elderly passengers”, adding that buses pose “the greatest risk to cyclists”, so London’s 164 bus stop bypasses – the official name for floating bus stops – provide a safer route “for everyone by physically separating buses from people on bikes”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Chinese car giant plots Jaguar Land Rover tie-up after opening UK base
data.description:
A Chinese car giant has paved the way for a potential tie-up with Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) after announcing plans to establish a European headquarters in Britain.

Chery, which makes Jaecoo and Omoda vehicles, will launch a new base in Liverpool with the company expected to use the hub to “integrate deeply into the UK automotive ecosystem”.

The site – which will be a centre for management, research and commercial development – is expected to become a focal point for any regional partnerships the Chinese firm strikes, local officials said.

It comes days after Chery was reported to be considering a tie-up that could see it produce cars at one of JLR’s plants in the UK.

That proposal will be a topic of discussions during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to China this week, according to the Financial Times.

Gong Yueqiong, general manager of Chery Commercial Vehicle, said: “We are not just bringing products to the UK – we are building a British business.

“Our strategy reflects our belief that true globalisation comes from deep localisation.”

He added: “By recruiting local talent, collaborating with UK institutions and adapting to local needs, we aim to become a valued contributor to the UK’s automotive industry and economy.”

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, hailed the decision as “a major vote of confidence in the UK, showcasing exactly the kind of high value, future focused manufacturing we are determined to grow”.

A spokesman for JLR declined to comment on suggestions of a partnership.

Chery has seen its sales in Britain surge lately. In 2025, the combined UK market share of the two brands leapt from just 0.2pc to 2.4pc after sales rocketed from 3,800 to 48,000.

The Jaecoo 7 SUV – dubbed the “Temu Range Rover” by some fans – has enjoyed particular success, becoming one of the best-selling electric cars.

Jaecoo is just one of the low-cost Chinese carmakers posing a threat to Western brands, with upstarts such as BYD and Leapmotor growing their market share.

The Liverpool announcement will fuel speculation that Chery could now seek to tap production capacity at JLR’s nearby Halewood factory to produce cars locally as well.

Tom Jervis, of Auto Express said the move marked “a major moment for Britain’s car industry”, adding: “It may open the door to the rumoured deal with Jaguar Land Rover to build cars at the nearby Halewood factory, which would bring a further timely boost to domestic manufacturing, while strengthening Chery’s foothold in the British market.

“From an industrial perspective, UK production makes strong sense. It would enable Chery to avoid tariffs and long supply chains, help anchor manufacturing in Britain as the shift to electrification accelerates and give flexibility as Chery currently offers petrol, hybrid and electric models.

“If successful, this partnership could point the way forward for how the UK sustains car production into the next decade.”

Liam Robinson, leader of Liverpool City Council, said: “Chery Commercial Vehicle’s choice of Liverpool for its commercial vehicle European base is a major vote of confidence in our city’s capabilities, our workforce and our long-term vision for clean, inclusive growth.

“This partnership has the potential to reshape the future of the car manufacturing sector in our city region – bringing new technologies, new skills and new opportunities to Liverpool and beyond.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ranked: The best and worst UK cities for public transport
data.description:
Rapid transit was born in the United Kingdom, with the opening of the Metropolitan Railway in 1863, followed by lines in Liverpool in 1886 and Glasgow in 1896. Since that heady period, only one new city has received a dedicated urban network, with the Tyne and Wear Metro, serving Newcastle, Gateshead and Sunderland, opening in 1980.

Metro rail systems have been repeatedly tabled by local authorities, and ruled out, in Belfast, Sheffield, Bristol, Cardiff, Cambridge, Birmingham and Manchester. In December 2025, the planned £2.5bn West Yorkshire Mass Transit tram scheme connecting Leeds and Bradford was postponed indefinitely. The highly anticipated, long-stalled £17bn Northern Powerhouse Manchester-Liverpool rail line has also been shunted into the hazy future.

Buses remain the chief mode for moving locals, and visitors, around UK cities. Manchester’s city-owned Bee Network is expanding, with fares capped on all buses, trains and trams, and free electric buses operating in the city centre. The model is London, where buses were never deregulated and the number of journeys has risen from 1.2 billion in 1986 to 1.8 billion today. Elsewhere, usage has dive-bombed.

Cars still play a role in UK cities and self-drive cabs could be the biggest disruptors yet. Clean-air charging zones have been introduced in London, Bath, Birmingham, Bradford, Bristol, Portsmouth, Sheffield, and Tyneside. However, London is still ranked 14th cleanest in Europe by the Clean Cities Campaign and not a single UK city appears in the global top 20 of cycling cities, assessed by the Copenhagenize Index (Bristol, Manchester, Glasgow and London are, at least, in the top 100).

It is, then, a mixed picture. Here’s my take on 12 transport systems for major cities in the UK, ranked from worst to best. Please let us know your thoughts and share your experiences in the comments.

12. Bristol

My verdict: 2/10

Forums, news reports and rankings concur that the gateway-city to the west of England is easier to whizz through than to explore.

In 2018 – after 12 years of local government dithering – MetroBus was launched as an affordable alternative to trams. High-spec guided buses would fly along dedicated busways around Bristol and the surrounding area, providing rapid transit for a fraction of the cost of a tram network. To date, more than £230m has been spent developing the scheme, and while it generates fewer angry headlines than a couple of years ago, the buses are often late, slow moving, overcrowded and are considered expensive by local users and students.

Bristol has no metro-type rail service, with GWR trains stopping at only a handful of suburban stations. Strike action by drivers of First buses in September 2025 caused widespread disruption, but was suspended in October following a pay settlement.

In 2008, Bristol was named as the UK’s first Cycling City, backed by £11.4m of government funding, and is still regarded as a good city for cyclists, despite its steep slopes. Oh, and its clogged roads: Bristol was the second most congested city in the UK in transport and traffic tech firm Inrix’s 2025 survey.

11. Sheffield

My verdict: 3/10

The Sheffield Supertram is a light rail system with 50 stations and three main lines (Blue, Yellow, Purple) connecting the city centre with the universities and suburbs like Hillsborough and Crystal Peaks. Fares for 2026 have been capped at £3 for the first time in 25 years, pandemic years excluded.

But as recently as December, the service was in crisis mode, with the Sheffield Tribune reporting: “As passengers will know, over the last few weeks, the service has been terrible. So bad, in fact, that the mayor and his team went out to tram stops last Friday to apologise and give people free tickets and chocolate.”

Locals, fed up with cancellations due to broken overhead lines and staff shortages, are giving up on the light railway service – brought back under public control in 2024 – instead returning to buses. But evening bus services have seen patchy performance, too, while some areas are poorly served and estate-touring routes take forever. The South Yorkshire Mayoral Authority aims to run a publicly controlled bus network from September 2027.

Last July, the Government “paused” plans to electrify more of the Midland Mainline, making this Britain’s largest city without electrified rail travel.

10. Leeds

My verdict: 4/10

It’s often claimed – especially by Yorkshire’s politicians – that Leeds is the largest city in Europe without a metro, tram or light rail. But the departures board at the main rail terminal is packed with commuter-type services and Northern runs 15 separate lines out of the station. Residents of Ilkley, Keighley, Castleford and Skipton benefit from regular trains to the city.

West Riding is a constellation of cities and towns, and there are regular and frequent trains to Wakefield, Sheffield and Hull. Nonetheless, according to the Centre for Cities: “Although Leeds and Marseille have comparable populations… the French city has around 776,000 residents who can access the city centre by public transport in 30 minutes or less, while 299,000 people can reach Leeds city centre under the same criteria.” That is, admittedly, not great.

West Yorkshire came 39th out of 39 for bus services in Transport Focus’s 2024 bus passenger satisfaction survey – for the second year running: “Arriva in West Yorkshire was the lowest rated bus operator with 69 per cent of passengers satisfied with their journeys.”

9. Glasgow

My verdict: 5/10

The third oldest underground system in the world – after London and Budapest – the Glasgow Metro opened in 1896. Sadly, the 6.5-mile network – if that’s even the word – hasn’t been expanded since, and so the vast majority of the 1.85 million people who call metropolitan Glasgow home live nowhere near a station.

Perhaps the track could do with some modernising, too, as cold weather led to two days of power outages earlier this month. In 2024, the network’s iconic “Clockwork Orange” trains, introduced in the Eighties, were replaced by new, modern, wheelchair-accessible trains, as part of a £288m system upgrade – which will also feature driverless trains, updated signalling, and platform screen doors.

On the access front, Centre for Cities approves: “All large British cities, except Glasgow, have worse public transport accessibility than their European peers”.

Most Glaswegians rely on the bus, but the network is generally rated so-so – with the main operator, First Bus, scoring a dismal 1.5 stars on Trustpilot. “Absolutely shocking service, this company is the definition of bare minimum,” said one local user.

8. Manchester

My verdict: 5.5/10

Whatever you think of Andy Burnham as a metro mayor, you have to admit he’s good at marketing. He has turned championing the Bee bus network into a powerful political vehicle. Manchester began taking over its buses in 2023, a process completed on January 5 – the first city to do so in the UK, following 40 years of deregulation.

Joint tram and bus travel is now 20 per cent cheaper with a new AnyBus + tram ticket. Adult fares were capped at £2 for all Greater Manchester bus services in 2022. Fifty new electric buses operate in the first franchised area, with hundreds more due to come over the next few years. Buses will run earlier, later and more frequently, with integrated bus services connecting passengers with first and last tram and train services.

The missing link in the masterplan, though, is Lancashire: metro systems really need to reach beyond official city borders and some services into the county are slow, unreliable and infrequent. The Ordsall Chord, opened in 2017, has helped connect central Manchester rail stations, but draughty, smelly, diesel-heavy Victoria still feels like a blast from the past.

Other bad smells are the ego-shrivelling withdrawal of HS2 and more than £100m in taxpayer funding blown on the cancelled clean air zone in Greater Manchester. The city and its sprawling suburbs consistently rank worst or near to the worst in air pollution surveys.

7. Birmingham

My verdict: 6/10

Britain’s second most populous city has no dedicated rail or underground trains. The West Midlands Railway – which actually extends to Manchester and Hereford – is the local name for West Midlands Trains, which also operates to London Euston and Liverpool Lime Street under a separate brand name – London Northwestern Railway. But there is a fairly frequent service between Birmingham and the main hubs at Wolverhampton, Walsall and Coventry.

A single 15-mile tram line connects Wolverhampton and Birmingham and the system is being expanded to 80 stops as part of a £1.3bn investment programme. Birmingham’s bus and coach connections were ranked second in transport co-ordination firm CMAC’s 2024 ranking of the best UK cities for public transport, with the judges highlighting “the city’s focus on sustainable and efficient transport solutions, such as introducing low-emission buses and real-time tracking technology.”

6. Belfast

My verdict: 6.5/10

When the Northern Irish capital’s new Grand Central station opened in September 2024, it was welcomed by locals – the old “integrated terminal” at the Europa Bus Centre was pretty awful – but universally described as not at all grand. Certainly, compared with the New York original, and many European city termini, the sawtooth glass structure is visually underwhelming. Frequent trains operate to Bangor, but most rail services are inter-city, leaving every 20 minutes or so, or every hour to Dublin.

There are a few stops inside the city limits, but the main metro system is the extensive network of pink city buses, operated by Translink, covering 12 main routes with a high level of frequency, connecting the city centre to suburbs and beyond. These 12 splinter into lettered branches, which means less frequency if you’re going further away. There’s also the Glider BRT service, which consists of two routes operated by articulated buses, using 15 miles of dedicated bus lanes.

5. Cardiff

My verdict: 7/10

The “Welsh Tube” is coming! The South Wales Metro, a £1bn transport system to be run by Transport for Wales, should be up and running from spring 2026, with 105 miles of largely overground travel, linking the Welsh capital with visitor attractions at Caerphilly and Barry Island.

Greener, quieter tram-trains will run on six electrified, colour-coded lines, with new stations, better access and a contactless payment system. The network should deliver a train every 15 minutes from Merthyr, Aberdare and Treherbert to Cardiff, and a train every 3-4 minutes from Pontypridd to Cardiff. Journey times from the top of each valley into Cardiff will also be reduced by around 15-20 minutes, making journeys faster.

Plans are also pushing forward for Cardiff Crossrail, an on-street tram service linking Cardiff Central with Cardiff Bay. It hopes to be operational when the city plays host to the 2028 European Football Championship. Cardiff’s buses are fair to middling, but are criticised for being hub-and-spoke; going in and out of the city is easy, but anywhere else difficult to impossible.

4. Liverpool

My verdict: 7.5/10

With just six underground stations and two lines, Liverpool’s dedicated metro – born in 1886 – may first appear very wee indeed. But the 68 stations covering 75 miles are part of one of the most heavily used railway networks in the UK outside London.

Merseyrail’s network reaches out to Southport, Ormskirk, the Wirral and Chester, with Northern and other firms running to St Helens, Warrington and Manchester on the so-called “City line”. It’s a highly rated system, topping Transport Focus satisfaction rankings in 2025.

In 2023, a new station, Headbolt Lane, was built at Kirkby, but onward journeys to Wigan and Blackburn involve a change. Most UK rail operators lease their trains, a largely unreported spanner in the works for so-called nationalisation. But Merseyrail’s new Class 777 trains are owned by the Liverpool City Region Combined Authority, marking a shift towards direct ownership for future fleet upgrades and expansion.

Buses in Liverpool were rated above Manchester’s in the Transport Focus 2024 passenger satisfaction survey, but still only 28th out of 39. Given all the hoo-ha around the Bee network, we can expect Liverpool to rebrand its buses any day now...

Since December, Avanti trains to and from London have been stopping every hour at Liverpool South Parkway, allegedly to ease trips to the airport. But that’s a two-mile walk with your luggage. For now, the bus is better.

3. London

My verdict: 8/10

The Elizabeth Line, which opened in 2022 and cost £19bn, has put a spring in the step of London’s commuters, especially those living on an east-west axis. Ongoing improvements on the six lines of the London Overground network, which has expanded hugely since its launch in 2007, have eased movements outside the central zone.

Old Tube trains on the Bakerloo and Piccadilly lines are still unpleasant when jam-packed, but a lot of rolling stock is being replaced. Work from home and less footfall on Fridays has made the Tube less of a sweating-sardine experience.

With around 700 bus routes (servicing a network that was, uniquely for the UK, never fully deregulated), fast and frequent trains to outlying dormitory towns (under 50 mins to Peterborough) and the nation’s only international rail link, London is by many miles the most extensive and best-funded urban transport system in the UK.

It’s £3,264 for a six-zone annual travelcard; a chunk of money, for sure, but driving is a no-no these days and, in any case, according to Inrix, London is the most congested city in Europe.

2. Nottingham

My verdict: 8.5/10

Assorted rankings for “least car-dependent city” have often praised Nottingham, due to its extensive bus network and 20 miles of tram lines.

Nottingham City Transport scored the highest customer satisfaction score of any city operator in the country in the UK Bus Operator of the Year 2024; it was the sixth time the city was given the title. Transport Focus’s Your Bus Journey survey for 2024 also praised Nottinghamshire and Greater Nottingham, which came sixth and seventh in its list of 39 local authorities.

“Nottingham City Council has concentrated its efforts on improving the overall network for passengers... [including] better bus stops and shelters and faster journey times,” it said.

The Robin Hood pay-as-you-go card can be used across Nottingham’s bus, tram and train network, making it simple and hassle-free to get around Nottingham. A range of ticket types is available, including a paper day-ticket bought on board buses or at tram stops. Locals want the trams to be expanded and the home of Raleigh and Sturmey-Archer gears remains a work in progress for cyclists, but this city seems to be moving forward on several right paths.

1. Edinburgh

My verdict: 9/10

In 2015, the Border Railways line between Edinburgh and Tweedbank opened, the first new domestic passenger service in a century. It’s very scenic, like so many journeys in Scotland.

Edinburgh lacks a metro rail network, but does it need one? The Central Belt is pretty well served in most areas, and Edinburgh has decent connections to its main exurbs. For tourists, the city is eminently walkable, notwithstanding the slopes of Old Town. There are regular buses to attractions like the Royal Botanic Gardens, Edinburgh Zoo and Portobello Beach.

The tram line, which generated heaps of negative press before it opened in 2014 due to cost overruns, delays, a contract cancellation and disruption caused by roadworks, is now considered a major positive by most locals. With 23 stops over 11.5 miles, it connects Prince Street with Leith and the airport. Trams run every 7 minutes for most of the day, and every 10 minutes early mornings and evenings. They don’t run through the night, but the Lothian NightBus, launched in 1925, is legendary.

Ticket prices in Edinburgh are competitive – £2.20 for an adult single on the bus or tram anywhere in the city zone.

In March 2025, Time Out ranked Edinburgh among the world’s best for public transport, behind only London and Brighton in the UK. It reported that 80 percent of locals expressed “a positive view of its transport network”. The Edinburgh tram is much more enjoyable than the Tube, though, and, frankly, the views are better from its buses – so I have the Scottish capital pipping the English one.

Data sources: Transport Focus; Clean Cities Campaign; CMAC; Centre for Cities; Time Out; Copenhagenize; UK Bus Awards; Britain Remade; Inrix; Trustpilot.

Do you agree with our expert’s ranking? Let us know in the comments below.

data.title: China unleashes fracking boom to ramp up gas supplies
data.description:
China is on track to become the world’s third-largest gas producer after rapidly expanding its fracking capabilities.

In an attempt to reduce the economy’s reliance on imported energy, Beijing is seeking to emulate the shale gas revolution that has propelled the US to energy independence.

Initial efforts have already led to China’s natural gas production hitting a record high last year, rising 6pc to 262 billion cubic metres.

This has drawn China level with Iran, with only the US and Russia producing more.

However, current trends signal China will overtake Iran this year, with president Xi Jinping seeking to increase gas production to 300 billion cubic metres by 2030.

China’s shale gas output has already grown by about 20pc a year since 2017, having started fracking in 2012.

Last year, the cumulative amount of shale gas extracted surpassed 100 billion cubic metres.

China still gets about 40pc of its gas from overseas.

Russia pumps about 38 billion cubic metres a year into China via pipeline, while also shipping in about 10pc of China’s annual intake of liquefied natural gas (LNG), mostly from a Western-sanctioned project in the Arctic.

This alliance with Russia is expected to grow in the coming years, after Xi promised Vladimir Putin in September that China would buy an additional six billion cubic metres of pipeline gas this year.

In 2027, China will also buy 12 billion cubic metres via a pipeline from Russia’s eastern Sakhalin Island.

The two leaders have also committed to a new pipeline that could ultimately supply about 50 billion cubic metres of gas through Mongolia.

China is the world’s largest importer of LNG. Its other key suppliers are Australia, Qatar and Malaysia.

The US was previously a supplier, but Chinese tariffs, which are still in force, have halted this trade.

China now wants to emulate the US energy sector, rather than buy from it.

America’s shale revolution kicked off in the early 2000s, as technological innovation and rapid exploration powered huge increases in output. In the five years to 2019, US production expanded by 223 billion cubic metres.

China’s shale geology, based around Sichuan in central China and Ordos in Inner Mongolia, is more challenging than America’s.

But the potential dwarfs that of its rivals. China has 1,100 trillion cubic feet of estimated shale gas reserves, compared with 600 trillion cubic feet in the US.

Beijing has been offering tax breaks and subsidies to its two state-owned energy giants, CNPC and Sinopec, to kickstart the revolution.

This is in stark contrast to the UK, where fracking has failed to take off under both Labour and the Conservatives.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, last year announced plans to introduce a permanent ban on fracking.

China’s overall gas output has now drawn level with that of Iran, which produced 263 billion cubic metres in 2024.

The only two bigger producers that year, according to the Energy Institute, were the US (one trillion cubic metres) and Russia (630 billion cubic metres).

Even with a fracking boom, China will not become a gas exporter. It expects to consume 550 billion cubic metres of gas a year by 2030, about 80pc more than it would produce.

Gas accounts for about 9pc of China’s total energy demand, but is expected to play an increasingly important role alongside renewable energy as the country seeks to wean itself off coal.

data.title: How Britain banned beauty
data.description:
What we know today as the classical Georgian terrace emerged from a catastrophe.

In the wake of the Great Fire of London in 1666, Parliament passed a series of acts to change the way houses were designed. 

This meant the capital went from having narrow, cramped streets full of overhanging Tudor homes – complete with wooden beams and eaves – to more uniform, elegant and brick-built terraces. 

This triggered what is regarded as Britain’s first speculative building boom, with developers throwing up streets of houses using new construction techniques.

Hundreds of years later, Georgian homes are still beloved by most Britons with polls consistently showing they are actually preferred over new housing.

An Ipsos Mori survey in 2015 found Georgian-style homes were the most popular, preferred by 75pc, while research by Anglian Home Improvements in 2019 found that Georgian architecture was Britain’s “most desirable” property style.

In 2023, research by the Chartered Institute of Building also concluded that most buyers viewed older homes as higher quality. Famous neighbourhoods known for their Georgian homes, such as Clifton in Bristol and Bedford Square in London, are among the country’s most sought-after locations. 

Whereas modern developments can feel lacking in character, Georgian properties are known for their ornamental features: pediments and cornicing, pillars and panelled walls, attractive quoining and wrought iron railings.

“Georgian terraced homes are light-filled, they feel natural, they've got pattern, variety and a little bit of joy,” says Nicholas Boys-Smith, of design consultancy Create Streets.

“They might have a fan window, a bit of texture, but they’ve also got a dignity and a sort of symmetry and order to them. 

“It's a good compromise between our brain’s need for order and our love of ornament and detail and playfulness.”

It’s a far cry from what many people find today when they tour one of Britain’s modern housing estates. 

Home buyers are often faced with streets of drab and unappealing new-builds. 

We can all picture these homes in our minds. They are carbon copies of each other, scattered across sprawling new neighbourhoods. The Chartered Institute of Building’s 2023 survey found two fifths of the public believed modern designs suffered from a “lack of character”.

“Surely, part of the function of a building is not just to provide shelter to those who live or use it internally, but also to bring joy and dignity to the much greater number of people who are going to pass by it,” says Boys-Smith. 

“So having a boxy and ugly building that is discourteous to the landscape and careless of a local tradition is thus actually not performing part of its function for the people who live nearby.”

Politicians from David Cameron to Sir Keir Starmer have repeatedly promised to make new developments more attractive. 

Some, including former housing secretary Michael Gove, have even argued that making homes beautiful is the antidote to Nimbyism. 

Yet instead, things seem to be getting worse. For instance, large windows are the most sought-after feature for house-hunters, according to Anglian Homes Improvement, but they keep getting smaller. Why?

One answer, according to experts, is that it is now all but impossible to build the kind of classical homes many of us love.

Through layer upon layer of well-meaning red tape, Britain has effectively banned beauty.

In 2006, then opposition leader David Cameron gave a speech criticising the way new developments looked and vowed to provide “homes that are beautiful in communities that are sustainable”.

However, 17 years later – and 13 years into government – the Tories were still railing against the standard of housing being delivered, with Michael Gove warning it was too often “ugly or identikit”. 

Clearly, no politician ever set out to encourage the building of eyesores across Britain. 

Yet according to experts, that is what successive governments have accidentally done with a blizzard of regulations.

Take windows, for example. Under rules first introduced under the Major government, upstairs windows have to be either 1.1 metres from the floor or guarded so people can't fall out of them. 

Just how necessary this protection is may be open to debate. The Government does not publish up-to-date figures on how many people die from falling out of windows, but the number is thought to be very small. 

Figures from the Office for National Statistics suggest an average of 77 people died per year after falling from windows, balconies, roofs, and walls from 2015 to 2024. However, this included the likes of builders who work at height.

Thirteen children died from falling out of windows between April 2019 and May 2025, according to Bristol University's National Child Mortality Database.

Many developers overcame these window safety rules by restricting how far they could open. But in 2022, then-housing secretary Michael Gove introduced rules that said windows must also open far enough to ventilate the room with fresh air during hot weather. This effectively ruled out window restrictors and meant developers switched to using smaller, squatter windows to comply with both rules.

Some get around this by putting bars over the windows, costing more than £200 per window, which many homeowners later remove anyway. 

Ironically, the regulations mean sash windows – famously good at creating draughts with their top and bottom openings – cannot be included in new builds in certain areas such as London which are deemed to be at “higher risk of overheating”. Getting them might be possible through carrying out expensive heat dynamics surveys, but many developers don’t want to do this. 

Samuel Hughes, of the Centre for Policy Studies, says the rules are a particularly bad example of “paternalistic” overreach by the Government. 

“It's amazing to me that building safety regulators think it's their job to tell British people what size of windows they ought to want,” he says. 

“People know how windows work.”

Meanwhile, other regulations dictate the proportions of homes in ways that rule out classical Georgian styles. 

Rules updated in 2004 and 2015 say all new homes must have step-free access, a lavatory suitable for wheelchairs on the ground floor and wider doors and hallways.

They are designed to make homes more accessible to people with disabilities – but tend to rule out popular features such as steps to the front door, while preventing the narrower, more space-efficient layouts of Georgian terraces. 

Minimum back-to-back distances between properties also mean houses must be spaced further apart. This results in developers being able to build fewer homes than before on the same patch of land. While Georgian homes can span densities of anywhere between 40 to 175 homes per hectare, most modern developments will only have 20 to 30 per hectare, according to Create Streets. 

That is partly because Georgian-style properties may lack driveways or have smaller gardens. Take these two examples: The city street with Georgian homes has twice as many homes as the rural new build development. 

Despite these drawbacks on driveways and gardens, Boys-Smith of Create Streets argues that such homes are nicer places to live overall.

“The great advantage of slightly higher density is that you just don't need to build on as much farmland to provide the homes we need,” he says.

“But also, you create a neighbourhood which is actually more sustainable because it’s easier to walk and cycle more if the distance to the nearest shop or pub or school is smaller.”

There is nothing inevitable about the kind of housing Britain has ended up with. Look internationally and you can find modern examples of classical-style housing that prioritises attractiveness.

In the Parisian suburb of Clamart, for example, a regeneration project over the last decade has seen blocks of flats built in the instantly recognisable 19th century Haussmann style, with architects central to the project.

As the Government prepares to build 12 new towns across England, beauty risks being overlooked. But Boys-Smith and others believe there is a modern template worth studying: Poundbury.

A traditionalist “urban extension” to Dorchester, Poundbury was the King’s passion project – intended as a rebuke to modernist architecture. 

There, designers found clever ways to incorporate traditional Georgian styles into modern homes – even within current planning rules and regulations. 

For example, many homes in Poundbury still feature sash windows. Designers complied with the regulations by making sure they only opened from the top and in some cases by installing bars.

A large number of houses also have steps to the front door, despite accessibility regulations. This is because side or back doors have been made step-free instead.

When he unveiled the new towns programme last year, Sir Keir Starmer hailed Poundbury as an example of the “gentle density” the Government wants to emulate in future developments. 

It’s a formula that has proved popular – and rewarding – for local residents. 

According to Create Streets, homes in Poundbury will sell at 55pc more value per hectare of development than other areas. Researchers said this is because people are willing to pay more to live in such properties.

Blake Holt, a local resident who moved to Poundbury from the countryside, says more developers should follow the scheme’s example.

The 78-year-old grandfather and his wife love how walkable the development is, as well as its varied and interesting architecture. 

“Having seen some other more recent estates that are being built, they look awful. They’re boxy and don’t feel like attractive places to live,” he says.

“In Poundbury, we love the architecture. It’s the way everything fits together. Everything is designed in a way that makes for much more attractive streetscapes.

“I think developers have a lot to learn from it.”

The Government insisted that building regulations do not completely ban features such as sash windows but admitted they do preclude them in places such as London where properties are “at higher risk of overheating”.

A spokesman for the ministry of housing and local government said: “Our landmark new towns will be attractive, well designed places where people want to live. 

“There is no ban on sash windows – or any type of window – and just today we have launched new design guidance to make sure we’re building homes and neighbourhoods people want to live in.

“This Government is taking action to fix the housing crisis we inherited and build 1.5 million homes to restore the dream of home ownership.”

Beauty isn’t completely banned yet. But unless Britain rediscovers its classical flair on a much grander scale, it may as well be.
