data.title: BBC shuts down investigations team in battle to slash costs
data.description:
The BBC has shut down a team dedicated to rooting out online fraud as part of cost-cutting efforts.

BBC Trending, which was tasked with investigating fake and fraudulent material on social media, will close despite the broadcaster’s attempts to counter disinformation online.

The move is expected to result in around four job losses, with affected staff informed of the cuts late last year.

BBC Trending was launched in 2013 by Mukul Devichand, now a senior editor at the New York Times. Dubbed the BBC’s “bureau on the internet”, it published online investigations and produced a weekly radio show on the World Service.

Recent stories included an investigation into a network of fraudsters profiting from fake AI-generated images of Holocaust victims, as well as exposing an elaborate scam in which criminals posed as Chinese police officers.

The decision to shut down the team, first reported by industry magazine Deadline, comes amid broader cost-cutting at the BBC as it attempts to plug a £500m black hole in its finances caused by a drop in licence fee revenues.

The public service broadcaster last year said it would cut 130 jobs from the World Service in an effort to save £6m.

That followed broader cuts across BBC News, including the closure of programmes such as the Hardtalk interview show.

Growing concerns over misinformation

However, the cutbacks will raise concerns about the BBC’s ability to counter a surge of misinformation online.

The corporation has cited this as a key factor behind its recent deal to make shows specifically for YouTube.

It said a range of new channels and live streams on the platform would provide “news that cuts through the noise”.

A source close to the BBC insisted that fighting disinformation remained an “absolutely critical” part of the broadcaster’s role. They pointed to recent investment in BBC Verify, its flagship fact-checking unit.

However, the BBC has itself been accused of spreading misinformation through its output.

A leaked whistleblowing report obtained by The Telegraph found that BBC Verify was forced to withdraw a story that incorrectly claimed car insurance companies were racist.

That was part of a broader catalogue of complaints about the BBC’s coverage, including a Panorama episode that misleadingly edited Donald Trump’s speech during the Jan 6 Capitol riots.

The scandal prompted the resignation of both Tim Davie, the director-general, and Deborah Turness, the head of news, while the US president has filed a $10bn (£7.3bn) defamation lawsuit against the BBC.

The closure comes as the BBC enters critical negotiations with ministers over the future of the licence fee ahead of the end of the current Charter period next year.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she is considering a range of possible reforms including funding the broadcaster through advertising or subscriptions.

In an interview with The Guardian this week, Mr Davie acknowledged that the BBC faced “profound jeopardy” unless it embraced an overhaul of its funding model. 

However, he dismissed both advertising and subscriptions, arguing that the BBC should remain a universal service.

data.title: Pensioner unable to call ambulance because BT landline cut
data.description:
A pensioner who suffered a stroke was unable to call an ambulance when their BT landline stopped working.

Graham Chapman, 75, from Horsehay in Shropshire, fell ill earlier this month. When his wife Jan went to call for help, she realised the phone was “completely dead”, the BBC reported.

The couple’s digital landline had been rendered useless after an error in upgrading their broadband.

The telecom giant is one of many rolling out “Digital Voice”, a system that has scrapped traditional copper landlines and made all phone lines reliant on broadband routers.

Mr and Mrs Chapman had already switched over to the Digital Voice system, which was working prior to their broadband order being cancelled.

When the plans were announced, they provoked concern that the elderly might be left isolated from emergency services, leading BT and Virgin Media O2 to pause deployment in 2023.

An overhaul of the landline system was planned to be completed by the end of 2025, but the deadline has been pushed back to 2027.

Engineers working on Mr Chapman’s home had been unable to connect them to fibre broadband on Jan 12 because they “hadn’t got enough cable”, but assured the pair “everything was working” fine when they left.

Later that week, Mr Chapman suffered a stroke. Mrs Chapman recalled going straight to the landline to ring 999.

“I felt really stressed out”, Mrs Chapman said, describing how she “lifted the phone and it was completely dead. I thought at the time, and I still do, that it’s the worst thing that’s happened to me.”

Fortunately, Mr Chapman was able to receive hospital care after a mobile phone call. He spent a week at Telford’s Princess Royal Hospital and is now recovering at home.

Ofcom has said it could investigate and fine BT if bungled landline cut-offs put customers at risk, after fining Virgin Media £23.8m in 2025 for its handling of the switch-over, saying it had put vulnerable customers at “direct risk of harm”.

The company admitted disconnecting customers who relied on life-saving “telecare” panic buttons to call for medical help.

Recovering from his medical emergency, Mr Chapman called the incident “absolutely scandalous” and said he could “foresee deaths occurring because of it”.

He claimed he had spent more than two weeks trying to have the landline fixed – a process he described as “absolutely terrible, frustrating” and as having affected his recovery.

A BT spokesman confirmed that the Chapmans’ landline has been restored.

They said: “We’re very sorry that the Chapmans’ landline phone was not working, following a broadband upgrade order. We have restored their landline service and confirmed that all is working.

The Telegraph previously revealed that changes to Digital Voice landlines had left a number of vulnerable customers without access to a phone line over Christmas.

Ofcom has said it could investigate and fine BT if bungled landline cut-offs linked to the rollout put customers at risk.

They fined Virgin Media £23.8m in 2025 for its handling of the switch-over, saying it had put vulnerable customers at “direct risk of harm”.

Anna Sabine, a Lib Dem MP and member of the Digital Communities all-party parliamentary group, previously told The Telegraph: “We warned that the safety net wasn’t there, and it is unacceptable that retirees and families in remote areas have paid the price for this botched rollout during the most isolating time of the year.”

A report published by the group in March 2025 warned that the switch-over could have “deeply personal repercussions” for vulnerable older and disabled people.

Chris Bryant, the telecoms minister, said in 2024: “This has kept me up at night and [is] a priority that I have put at the forefront of my work since stepping into office.

“I am pleased telecoms companies, central government, and local authorities are working in lockstep to achieve customer safety.”

A BT spokesman said the firm had been raising awareness of the switch-over for the past two years and that it had reviewed cases reported by The Telegraph earlier this month of vulnerable customers being cut off.

The spokesman added: “We’re very sorry that the Chapmans’ landline phone was not working, following a broadband upgrade order. We have restored their landline service and confirmed that all is working.

“The Chapmans will receive automatic compensation for the period they were unable to use their landline, and their complaint has been resolved.”

data.title: CityFibre to cut hundreds of jobs as debt pressures mount
data.description:
CityFibre is to cut hundreds of jobs as the debt-laden broadband firm braces for a wave of consolidation across the industry.

The British telecoms group, which is backed by Goldman Sachs and Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala fund, on Wednesday outlined plans to slash 450 roles, almost a third of its 1,400-strong workforce.

The company said the redundancies will predominantly affect engineers as it shifts focus from building a full-fibre network to buying up struggling rivals.

In a note to staff, Simon Holden, the chief executive, said CityFibre’s current structure was “no longer the right fit for where our business is heading” and sweeping cuts were needed.

He added: “This is not something we take lightly, and we are committed to working through it together. We believe these changes are necessary to ensure CityFibre continues to deliver for customers while remaining resilient and well-positioned for the future.”

The redundancies, which were first reported by the Financial Times, are the latest sign of cost-cutting in the challenger broadband market.

A host of firms dubbed “alt-nets” sprung up to rival BT, borrowing heavily to fund the construction of expensive full-fibre networks. However, surging interest costs and sluggish take-up have left many struggling to survive.

CityFibre, which has around 850,000 customers, last week said revenues hit a record £170m last year. Its performance has been boosted by a deal to host Sky customers on its network.

But the company omitted key details on the eye-watering cost of servicing its debts, which stand at almost £4bn.

Last year, the telecoms group secured a crucial £2.3bn refinancing following a lengthy stand-off between shareholders and lenders about who would pump in the money. It has said that the funding will last until the middle of next year.

CityFibre has positioned itself as the key challenger to BT’s Openreach and Virgin Media O2 and is now considered a likely suitor to sweep up an array of smaller alt-nets.

One of those, G.Network, recently collapsed into administration after being taken over by private equity firm FitzWalter Capital.

Gigaclear, a rural broadband provider, is also poised to be taken over by lenders, including NatWest and the taxpayer-backed National Wealth Fund.

A CityFibre spokesman said: “As we shift our strategy from building to acquiring networks, backed by our recent £2.3bn financing, the time is right to review how we operate so we are best placed to continue our success through this next phase of growth.

“All of our people have helped to get CityFibre into a very strong position and we will support our teams as we take this step and ensure CityFibre becomes the long-term, sustainable competitor the UK needs.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Black M&S worker claimed Project Coffee title was racist
data.description:
A black woman sued Marks & Spencer after claiming that a secret code name for its diversity programme was racist.

Claudia Royer sued for race discrimination after being left “unshakeably convinced” that Project Coffee was being used as the title of a drive to recruit more personal assistants from a diverse background.

She claimed it was a “racist trope” and took the high street chain to an employment tribunal.

However, the tribunal found it was actually the secretive name given to an M&S structural reorganisation.

Her “wild” race discrimination case was thrown out, with the judge ruling that Ms Royer had “lost touch with reality”.

The London Central Employment Tribunal heard that she had worked at M&S as a personal assistant for more than nine years. The hearing was told she believed she had been asked to greet new appointees because she was black.

“She told us in evidence that the initiative was given a secret code name of Project Coffee, which she regarded as a racist trope,” the tribunal ruling said.

“The case was that, in early 2019, it launched plans for a reorganisation under the code name Project Coffee. The reorganisation had nothing to do with the recruitment of PAs.”

In August 2023, personal assistants in the foods group at M&S were reorganised, and Ms Royer was made redundant in October that year.

In the same month, she wrote to the chief executive of M&S with several complaints about unfair and discriminatory treatment, and this was treated as a formal grievance.

‘Seemed to lose touch with reality’

Rachel Lowe, an M&S manager, found that Ms Royer was “quite mistaken” in her perception of the nature and purpose of Project Coffee. Ms Royer appealed against the dismissal of her grievance, but was not successful.

The tribunal found that her position on Project Coffee was one “which seemed to lose touch with reality and common sense”.

M&S brought a document relating to the reorganisation project, which had the words Project Coffee at the top, to the tribunal.

Employment Judge Anthony Snelson said: “Unfortunately, [Ms Royer] has become unshakeably convinced by her surprising theory that there was a secret programme called Project Coffee designed to make the cohort of PAs more racially representative of the world outside the [M&S] organisation.

“That [Ms Royer] should pursue the matter even after being shown a document which clearly evidences the fact that Project Coffee refers to a proposal for a structural reorganisation, resorting to the wild allegation that the document was manufactured, reflects poorly on her judgment.

“Nor was there any detriment in asking her to welcome new colleagues to the organisation. That was a task which fell naturally within her remit, and there could be no objection to being asked to perform it.”

The tribunal found that she was sacked because her role had become redundant. All her tribunal claims, including a claim for unfair dismissal, failed.

data.title: Banks target top 10pc of earners with best mortgage deals
data.description:
Banks have eased mortgage lending to allow buyers to borrow more, with the best deals reserved for higher earners.

Lenders targeting “Henrys” – which stands for “High Earner, Not Rich Yet” – have increased how many times their salary buyers can borrow.

NatWest and Nationwide announced these borrowers would be able to take mortgages of up to six times their salary.

But the caveat was that single borrowers would need to earn at least £75,000, and couples £100,000, in order to benefit from the more generous lending. For a buyer earning £75,000, putting them in the top 10pc of earners, this would mean being able to borrow an extra £37,500, NatWest said.

Nationwide will allow some existing mortgage customers to borrow more without the income requirement, and first-time buyers who will commit to longer term fixed-rates have lower income requirements.

The average UK salary was just over £39,000 in April last year, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

City regulators the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) relaxed rules about riskier lending last year, allowing borrowers with high incomes to take on larger mortgages.

Traditionally, banks have been hesitant to allow buyers to borrow more than 4.5 times their income – and larger lenders can still only offer 15pc of their mortgages at higher levels.

Henry Jordan, of Nationwide, said: “The government and regulatory changes last year have been a game changer for first-time buyers. Over the past year, we’ve seen a five-fold increase in the number of first-time buyers borrowing between 5.5 and six times income.”

Competition among lenders is expected to continue throughout the year, as lower inflation predictions mean that further cuts to the Bank Rate are expected. Mortgage rates fell over the course of last year as the Bank Rate came down, with the average two-year fixed rate dipping below 5pc for the first time since 2022 last August.

Nottingham Building Society announced earlier this month that it was cutting the earning requirements for its higher multiple mortgages, allowing those earning £65,000 a year to borrow up to 5.5 times their income.

Lender Santander allows those earning between £45,000 and £99,999 to borrow up to five times their income if they have a deposit of at least 10pc. But those earning more than £100,000 a year can borrow 5.5 times their income.

The bank will also allow high earners to have more generous interest-only mortgages. Those earning more than £200,000 can borrow up to 75pc of the value of their home on an interest-only basis – compared to just 50pc for those earning under that threshold.

HSBC now allows its Premier customers to borrow up to 6.5 times their salary. This means a borrower earning £100,000 could borrow £650,000, compared to £550,000 previously.

Adrian Anderson, of mortgage brokerage Anderson Harris, said: “Market factors are also helping banks to lend more. We have noticed as interest rates and mortgage rates have been reducing, the banks’ affordability stress testing has been easing.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Royal Mail tycoon takes £320m dividend after earning millions from wasted wind
data.description:
The billionaire owner of Royal Mail has taken a £320m dividend from his UK power business after it earned tens of millions of pounds from wasted wind.

EP UK Investments – which is controlled by Daniel Kretinsky, the Czech tycoon – paid out the sum in 2024, recently published accounts showed. His dividend rose from £50m the previous year.

Mr Kretinsky’s EP UK owns stakes in energy assets including power stations at South Humber Bank and Langage, Devon, as well as the Lynemouth biomass plant in Northumberland. It also acts as an energy trading company, buying and selling contracts for electricity, gas and carbon.

It is one of several energy giants paid to fire up gas power plants when Britain’s wind farms are switched off because of capacity issues.

Analysis for The Telegraph previously revealed that Mr Kretinsky’s company made £92m in 2024 from a practice known as “curtailment”, in which wind turbines are told to stop generating because the grid is too congested to accept their power.

The huge dividend risks reigniting a row over ballooning curtailment costs. Figures released this week showed Britain’s wind farm turbines wasted enough energy to power all of London’s homes last year.

Major suppliers including Octopus Energy have called for the country to be divided into different wholesale markets through a system known as zonal pricing, though experts have warned this would drive up bills for households in the South.

Mr Kretinsky’s £3.6bn takeover of Royal Mail last year was subject to intense political scrutiny and a national security review, owing to concerns about the 500-year-old postal service falling into foreign hands for the first time.

He is also the largest shareholder in Sainsbury’s and holds a 27pc stake in Premier League club West Ham United.

Accounts showed EP UK enjoyed a surge in pre-tax profits in 2024, rising from £99m the previous year to £152m, even as revenues halved to £1.1bn amid falling wholesale electricity prices.

Bosses said the improved profitability was a result of lower energy buying costs as the company reduced its power purchases, while trading commissions also increased.

The £320m dividend was paid to Luxembourg-based parent company EP Investments, which is controlled by Mr Kretinsky. The parent company also issued a £270m loan during the year to fund projects at its subsidiaries.

Mr Kretinsky has enjoyed booming profits from his British power assets in recent years as Russia’s invasion of Ukraine triggered chaos in energy markets.

Gas-fired power generators were exempted from the Government’s windfall tax, which was aimed at capping profits for energy firms as household bills soared. This was because they faced rising fuel costs as wholesale gas prices jumped.

The South Humber Bank and Langage plants have a combined capacity of around 2.3 gigawatts, capable of supplying power to around 1.5 million homes.

In the accounts, bosses said EP UK was continuing to explore further investment and development opportunities in Britain.

The holding company bought a power station in Corby, Northamptonshire, last year and increased its investment in the West Burton Energy power plant, near Gainsborough, Lincolnshire, which it runs as a joint venture with TotalEnergies.

However, Mr Kretinsky’s energy empire also scaled back plans to build a new gas-fired power station at Eggborough, North Yorkshire, in a setback to the back-up system designed to prevent blackouts in Britain.

data.title: Where to put £100k savings right now for the highest return
data.description:
Have you got a savings strategy to maximise your returns? We want to hear from you. Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Savings rates are very much on the decline. To protect your money from the damaging effects of inflation, it’s worth putting some effort into finding the accounts paying the best returns possible.

But with so many accounts to choose from, all with differing rates and conditions, finding the best home for your cash can be difficult. Everyone’s circumstances are different: some can afford to lock cash away for years, while others only feel comfortable if they can make instant withdrawals in case of emergencies.

Telegraph Money asked Katie Horne of Flagstone, a cash deposit platform, to give her views on three scenarios that represent common questions we get asked by readers.

For simplicity’s sake, we have not considered tax. In reality, tax on savings interest would be a problem for any money held outside an Isa.

Once you earn interest over £1,000 a year (for most basic-rate taxpayers) or £500 (for higher-rate payers), returns are subject to income tax, due to the personal savings allowance.

Scenario 1

A retired couple in their 70s with £100,000 in cash. They are happy to lock up £80,000 for more than a year, but need access to the rest – though not necessarily daily

Katie says: “The retired couple could take advantage of some longer-term fixed accounts, specifically the Is Bank UK 12-month Fixed Term Deposit, which pays 4.35pc.

“They can place £80,000 into this product, earning themselves interest of £3,480 a year. If they are comfortable securing a longer fixed term, and concerned that rates might be significantly lower in 12 months’ time, another option is Shawbrook Bank’s 2 Year Fixed Term Deposit, paying 3.92pc AER, which would generate £3,136 a year in interest.

“For the remaining £20,000, they could use their Isa allowance – Trading 212 Instant Access Cash Isa pays 4.38pc AER, generating interest of £876 a year.

“However, if they have used their Isa allowance, they could choose the Whiteaway Laidlaw (part of Shawbrook Bank) instant access account, paying 4.05pc AER, earning £810 in interest.

“All of these accounts would be covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. This balanced portfolio would meet their near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 3.94pc to 4.35pc, equivalent to between £3,946 and £4,356 a year.”

Scenario 2

A working woman in her 50s with £100,000 in cash. She needs ready access to £80,000 but can lock up £20,000 for up to three years

Katie says: “The lady could opt to place £80,000 into an instant-access account from Whiteaway Laidlaw, which pays 4.05pc AER.

“This account allows unlimited withdrawals without any restrictions and would earn her £3,240 in interest a year.

“If she is willing to restrict access somewhat, she could achieve a slightly higher rate with the Gatehouse Bank three-month fixed term product, currently paying 4.06pc AER, earning £3,248 in interest a year.

“For the remaining £20,000, she could secure the Chetwood Bank 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, currently paying 4pc, earning £800 a year in interest. Alternatively, she could lock in for a shorter period and secure a slightly higher rate of 4.35pc with the Is Bank UK 12 Month Fixed Term Deposit, earning £870 a year.

“Her selection here would probably be driven by her view on the likelihood of significantly lower savings rates in 12-18 months.

“This balanced portfolio would meet her near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.04pc to 4.11pc, or £4,040 to £4,118, again covered by the FSCS.”

Scenario 3

A working man in his 40s. Has no savings, but can put aside £1,500 a month for the next five years. Doesn’t need access for five years

Katie says: “A number of big banks offer good savings rates with specific requirements. For example, Santander Edge Saver offers 6pc AER with a minimum deposit of £1 and a maximum deposit of £4,000, or £233 a year in interest. Once his savings have grown, he can reassess and diversify his investments.

“We would also suggest that he look into cash Isas to take advantage of the tax-free status. You can currently invest up to £20,000 in Isas each year without paying tax on the interest you earn.

“The best rates over the past 12 months have been around the 4.5pc mark. Currently, Trading 212 offers a cash Isa at 4.38pc with no minimum required. If he puts in £300 a month, he’ll get a return of £87 a year.”

Scenario 4

A saver in their 60s with £100,000 in cash. They don’t need access to all of his money immediately, but want to generate a steady return while keeping some flexibility

Katie says: “For savers who want to strike a balance between flexibility and income, laddering fixed-term deposits can be a really effective strategy. It allows you to earn higher returns on money you don’t need straight away, while keeping some accessible in case of emergencies.

“In this example, £25,000 could be held in an easy access account with Whiteaway Laidlaw, paying 4.05pc AER, earning £1,012.50 in annual interest.

“A further £25,000 could be placed in a six-month fixed term account from Is Bank, paying 4.24pc AER, earning £1,060 in interest over the year.

“For the next portion, £25,000 could be locked into a 12-month fixed term deposit from Is Bank, paying 4.35pc, which would generate £1,087.50 in annual interest.

“The final £25,000 could go into a 24-month fixed term deposit from Shawbrook, paying 3.92pc AER, earning £980 a year.

“This combination would provide access to a portion of funds at all times, while maintaining steady income from staggered maturities. It also ensures full FSCS protection across four banks, a key advantage of spreading deposits.

“Altogether, this portfolio would achieve an average blended rate of 4.14pc, generating around £4,140 in annual interest, with funds maturing every 6 to 12 months to allow reinvestment at new market rates.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Crackdown on water companies ripping up roads after surge in disruption
data.description:
Water and energy companies will face penalties for ripping up London’s streets after a surge in roadworks worsened congestion across the capital.

Under plans put forward by Transport for London (TfL), utility firms will be charged for excavating roads and disrupting traffic during rush hour in a bid to minimise the impact on journey times.

The penalty scheme was previously trialled on London’s busiest roads, but will now be extended across streets in 26 boroughs, according to TfL.

It said: “The scheme charges utility companies and others a fee to dig up the busiest roads at the busiest times, reducing congestion and providing funds for innovative new ways to reduce delays on the roads.

“This has been proven to reduce congestion, improve journeys and enhance air quality.”

The extent of the charges will vary according to the road and will mostly apply during the busiest periods of the day, including between 6.30am and 9.30am, and 3.30pm and 7pm.

Daily fees will typically range from £350 for impeding a footpath to £2,500 for a main road.

Around 2.2 million street works were carried out in England between April 2023 and March 2024, with more than 200 organisations holding the right to dig up roads.

Works performed by utilities on water, gas, energy and broadband networks have increased by 30pc over the past decade, according to the Local Government Association.

Under the latest crackdown, Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, will take control of the penalty scheme from the Department for Transport.

Camden, Enfield, Lambeth and Merton have already received approval for their proposals to include more key routes in the scheme, while 22 other boroughs are working on applications.

A study carried out by transport analytics firm Inrix suggests that the number of planned roadworks in London has increased by almost 400pc since 2012, while journey times are lengthening by 12pc annually.

The study named London as Europe’s most congested city in 2024 and said the cost of congestion totalled £3.85bn, or £942 per driver. For the UK as a whole, the figure was £7.7bn, up £200m on the previous year.

‘Horrible cycle of roadworks’

Transport bosses last year complained to MPs that workmen were increasingly abusing emergency powers to dig up roads at little or no notice to avoid submitting more formal plans.

In evidence to the transport select committee, the Confederation of Passenger Transport, which represents bus operators, said its members were experiencing a surge in last-minute roadworks that gave them no time to prepare for lengthy traffic jams.

The AA said motorists were being subjected to a “horrible cycle of roadworks that never seem to end” and said utilities should be compelled to coordinate their activities rather than digging one hole after another in the same place.

TfL’s five-year roadworks plan also proposes the increased use of software that adjusts traffic light timings to reduce delays.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Mother of Barclay heir pursued for £19m debt
data.description:
The matriarch of the Barclay family is being pursued for debts of almost £19m owed by her son Alistair.

Deutsche Bank Luxembourg is seeking permission from the High Court to recover losses from Lady Reyna Barclay after Alistair Barclay failed to repay his debts.

Details of the bank’s pursuit have emerged after the private bank filed a claim against Alistair, 36, earlier this month.

Alistair is the son of Sir David Barclay, the former joint owner of The Telegraph and Lady Reyna.

Sir David, who died in 2021, and his twin brother, Sir Frederick Barclay, and their children built a multibillion-pound business empire that once included The Telegraph, online retailer Very and parcel delivery firm Yodel.

However, in recent years, the empire has imploded under heavy debts, triggering a string of legal battles involving the family.

High Court filings seen by The Telegraph show that Alistair, the youngest of Sir David’s four sons and a former motor racing driver, owes Deutsche Bank Luxembourg £18.7m after failing to repay a loan struck in 2022.

The German lender is suing Alistair and has targeted Vauvert Administration LBG, a Guernsey company listed as the trustee to two offshore trusts in his name.

However, Deutsche Bank is also pursuing Lady Reyna after Alistair said neither he nor his trusts can repay his debts, according to court papers.

‘Lady Barclay’s promise’

Lady Reyna allegedly agreed to serve as a guarantor to Alistair’s trusts. In its evidence to the court, Deutsche has referred to “Lady Barclay’s promise” to ensure Alistair’s trusts can “meet its obligations and liabilities as they fall due”.

Court filings from Deutsche state that “Lady Barclay is independently wealthy and thus capable of fulfilling her promise” to cover Alistair’s debts.

Lady Reyna’s alleged guarantee is said to expire in late March, and Deutsche Bank is seeking an emergency trial as it races to recoup its losses. Recovering the debts from Alistair’s mother is “realistically, the bank’s only prospect of substantial recovery”, Deutsche said in its filing.

“Since Mr Barclay’s default in 2023, the bank has engaged in lengthy efforts to arrive at a resolution,” it said. “Repeated attempts by the Bank to find a resolution have not succeeded.”

In his defence, Alistair has rejected claims that his mother’s “promise” was binding.

His lawyers told the court: “It is unexplained how Lady Barclay could conceivably be liable on her so-called ‘promise’. Parties cannot sue on a bare ‘promise’.

“The suggestion that the bank can take steps to enforce this ‘promise’ is a non-starter.”

Lawyers also claimed Deutsche Bank was attempting to use its case against Mr Barclay “as a hook to come after his mother”.

Alistair is seeking to move the case to the County Court, which Deutsche Bank said would lead to unnecessary delays.

Deutsche Bank has instructed Pinsent Masons. Alistair has hired Clarion Solicitors.

Deutsche Bank Luxembourg is the second private bank to initiate court proceedings against Alistair in two years.

In 2024, Investec’s private bank issued a bankruptcy petition over an allegedly overdue £1m loan, which was eventually settled out of court.

The lender hired investigators to track down Alistair, claiming he had been “actively avoiding” service of its claim.

He was found living in a Monaco seafront tower block called Le Roccabella with his mother. Investec claimed Mr Barclay had moved to the Mediterranean microstate after selling a grand townhouse in Chester Square, Belgravia.

Alistair’s half-brothers, Aidan and Howard, are facing their own legal battles after HSBC issued a bankruptcy petition against them.

This relates to the collapse of Yodel and its related companies, which owed the bank £140m. Aidan and Howard are alleged to have given personal guarantees on the loans.

Barclays’ financial troubles began in June 2023, when Lloyds Bank Group seized control of The Telegraph due to £1.2bn debt.

They repaid that debt in full after agreeing to hand control of The Telegraph to RedBird IMI, a vehicle majority-funded by the United Arab Emirates. The move was ultimately derailed by a cross-party outcry over press freedom.
